This meeting will be arranged by the court and will be done in a way that feels comfortable for the person identifying the arrested person, especially if they have a disability. The whole process will be recorded using audio or video equipment so that it can be used as evidence later.

subsections.text: Where a person is arrested on a charge of committing an offence and his identification by any other person or persons is considered necessary for the purpose of investigation of such offence, the Court, having jurisdiction may, on the request of the officer in charge of a police station, direct the person so arrested to subject himself to identification by any person or persons in such manner as the Court may deem fit: Provided that if the person identifying the person arrested is mentally or physically disabled, such process of identification shall take place under the supervision of a Magistrate who shall take appropriate steps to ensure that such person identifies the person arrested using methods that person is comfortable with and the identification process shall be recorded by any audio-video electronic means.

chapter: CHAPTER V: ARREST OF PERSONS
title: Procedure when police officer deputes subordinate to arrest without warrant.
content: (1) When any officer in charge of a police station or any police officer making an investigation under Chapter XIII requires any officer subordinate to him to arrest without a warrant (otherwise than in his presence) any 34 person who may lawfully be arrested without a warrant, he shall deliver to the officer required to make the arrest an order in writing, specifying the person to be arrested and the offence or other cause for which the arrest is to be made and the officer so required shall, before making the arrest, notify to the person to be arrested the substance of the order and, if so required by such person, shall show him the order. (2) Nothing in sub-section (1) shall affect the power of a police officer to arrest a person under section 35.
summary: When a police officer wants to arrest someone without a warrant, but can't be present himself, he must write an order specifying the person to be arrested and the reason for the arrest. This order must be given to the subordinate officer making the arrest. Before making the arrest, the subordinate officer must tell the person to be arrested why they are being arrested and show them the written order if they ask to see it. This ensures that the person being arrested knows why they are being arrested and has proof of the reason.

subsections.text: When any officer in charge of a police station or any police officer making an investigation under Chapter XIII requires any officer subordinate to him to arrest without a warrant (otherwise than in his presence) any 34 person who may lawfully be arrested without a warrant, he shall deliver to the officer required to make the arrest an order in writing, specifying the person to be arrested and the offence or other cause for which the arrest is to be made and the officer so required shall, before making the arrest, notify to the person to be arrested the substance of the order and, if so required by such person, shall show him the order.
Nothing in sub-section (1) shall affect the power of a police officer to arrest a person under section 35.

chapter: CHAPTER V: ARREST OF PERSONS
title: Health and safety of arrested person.
content: It shall be the duty of the person having the custody of an accused to take reasonable care of the health and safety of the accused.
summary: When someone is arrested, the person or agency in charge of them must make sure they are safe and healthy. This includes providing basic necessities like food, water, and medical care if needed. They must also protect the arrested person from any harm or injury.

subsections.text: It shall be the duty of the person having the custody of an accused to take reasonable care of the health and safety of the accused.

chapter: CHAPTER V: ARREST OF PERSONS
title: Person arrested to be taken before Magistrate or officer in charge of police station.
content: A police officer making an arrest without warrant shall, without unnecessary delay and subject to the provisions herein contained as to bail, take or send the person arrested before a Magistrate having jurisdiction in the case, or before the officer in charge of a police station.
summary: When a police officer arrests someone without a warrant, they must take that person to a Magistrate (a high-ranking government official) or the officer in charge of the local police station as soon as possible. This is to ensure the arrested person's rights are protected and to follow the law.

subsections.text: A police officer making an arrest without warrant shall, without unnecessary delay and subject to the provisions herein contained as to bail, take or send the person arrested before a Magistrate having jurisdiction in the case, or before the officer in charge of a police station.

chapter: CHAPTER V: ARREST OF PERSONS
title: Person arrested not to be detained more than twenty-four hours.
content: Officers in charge of police stations shall report to the District Magistrate, or, if he so directs, to the Sub-divisional Magistrate, the cases of all persons arrested without warrant, within the limits of their respective stations, whether such persons have been admitted to bail or otherwise.
summary: If a person is arrested without a warrant, the police officer in charge of the station where the arrest was made must inform the District Magistrate or the Sub-divisional Magistrate within 24 hours. This is to ensure that the arrested person is not detained for too long without being formally charged or granted bail.

subsections.text: Officers in charge of police stations shall report to the District Magistrate, or, if he so directs, to the Sub-divisional Magistrate, the cases of all persons arrested without warrant, within the limits of their respective stations, whether such persons have been admitted to bail or otherwise.

chapter: CHAPTER V: ARREST OF PERSONS
title: Police to report apprehensions.
content: Officers in charge of police stations shall report to the District Magistrate, or, if he so directs, to the Sub-divisional Magistrate, the cases of all persons arrested without warrant, within the limits of their respective stations, whether such persons have been admitted to bail or otherwise.
summary: When a police officer arrests someone without a warrant, they must inform the District Magistrate or the Sub-divisional Magistrate about the arrest. This report must include information about the person arrested, whether they were released on bail or not. The police officer is required to do this, even if the person arrested is later released.

subsections.text: Officers in charge of police stations shall report to the District Magistrate, or, if he so directs, to the Sub-divisional Magistrate, the cases of all persons arrested without warrant, within the limits of their respective stations, whether such persons have been admitted to bail or otherwise.

chapter: CHAPTER V: ARREST OF PERSONS
title: Discharge of person apprehended.
content: No person who has been arrested by a police officer shall be discharged except on his bond, or bail bond, or under the special order of a Magistrate.
summary:
When a police officer arrests someone, they can't just let them go without any conditions. The arrested person must either:

- Give a personal guarantee (called a bond) that they'll appear in court as required.
- Post a bail bond, which is like a guarantee from someone else that the arrested person will show up in court.
- Get a special order from a Magistrate (a high-ranking court official) to be released.

subsections.text: No person who has been arrested by a police officer shall be discharged except on his bond, or bail bond, or under the special order of a Magistrate.

chapter: CHAPTER V: ARREST OF PERSONS
title: Power, on escape, to pursue and retake.
content: (1) If a person in lawful custody escapes or is rescued, the person from whose custody he escaped or was rescued may immediately pursue and arrest him in any place in India. (2) The provisions of section 44 shall apply to arrests under sub-section (1) although the person making any such arrest is not acting under a warrant and is not a police officer having authority to arrest.
summary: If someone who is in lawful custody, like a prisoner, escapes or is let go, the person who was supposed to keep them in custody can immediately try to catch them anywhere in India. This person can even arrest them, even if they don't have a warrant and aren't a police officer. The rules for making an arrest in this situation are the same as if a police officer were making an arrest, but without the need for a warrant.

subsections.text: If a person in lawful custody escapes or is rescued, the person from whose custody he escaped or was rescued may immediately pursue and arrest him in any place in India.

The provisions of section 44 shall apply to arrests under sub-section (1) although the person making any such arrest is not acting under a warrant and is not a police officer having authority to arrest.

chapter: CHAPTER V: ARREST OF PERSONS
title: Arrest to be made strictly according to Sanhita.
content: No arrest shall be made except in accordance with the provisions of this Sanhita or any other law for the time being in force providing for arrest. CHAPTER VI PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE A.Summons
summary: According to this section of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, law enforcement agencies cannot arrest anyone without following the procedures laid down in this law or any other existing law that allows arrest. This means that before making an arrest, they must follow the correct steps and procedures as mentioned in the law.

subsections.text: No arrest shall be made except in accordance with the provisions of this Sanhita or any other law for the time being in force providing for arrest.
PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
Summons

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Form of summons.
content: Every summons issued by a Court under this Sanhita shall be, (i) in writing, in duplicate, signed by the presiding officer of such Court or by such other officer as the High Court may, from time to time, by rule direct, and shall bear the seal of the Court; or (ii) in an encrypted or any other form of electronic communication and shall bear the image of the seal of the Court or digital signature.
summary: When a court issues a summons under the BNSS, it must be in writing and have two copies. The court officer in charge must sign it, and the court's official seal must be on it. Alternatively, the summons can be sent electronically, such as through an encrypted email, and it must include a digital image of the court's seal or a digital signature to verify its authenticity.

subsections.text: Every summons issued by a Court under this Sanhita shall be, (i) in writing, in duplicate, signed by the presiding officer of such Court or by such other officer as the High Court may, from time to time, by rule direct, and shall bear the seal of the Court; or (ii) in an encrypted or any other form of electronic communication and shall bear the image of the seal of the Court or digital signature.
in writing, in duplicate, signed by the presiding officer of such Court or by such other officer as the High Court may, from time to time, by rule direct, and shall bear the seal of the Court
in an encrypted or any other form of electronic communication and shall bear the image of the seal of the Court or digital signature

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Summons how served.
content: (1) Every summons shall be served by a police officer, or subject to such rules as the State Government may make in this behalf, by an officer of the Court issuing it or other public servant: Provided that the police station or the registrar in the Court shall maintain a register to enter the address, email address, phone number and such other details as the State Government may, by rules, provide. 35 (2) The summons shall, if practicable, be served personally on the person summoned, by delivering or tendering to him one of the duplicates of the summons: Provided that summons bearing the image of Court's seal may also be served by electronic communication in such form and in such manner, as the State Government may, by rules, provide. (3) Every person on whom a summons is so served personally shall, if so required by the serving officer, sign a receipt therefor on the back of the other duplicate.
summary:
When a court issues a summons, it needs to be delivered to the person being summoned. 

The summons can be served by a police officer or a court officer. If the court makes rules for this, it can also be served by other public servants. 

The police station or the court must keep a record of the person's address, email, phone number, and other details. 

The summons can be handed to the person personally, or if that's not possible, it can be sent electronically with the court's seal. 

If the person is handed the summons personally, they might be asked to sign a receipt to confirm they received it.

subsections.text: Every summons shall be served by a police officer, or subject to such rules as the State Government may make in this behalf, by an officer of the Court issuing it or other public servant: Provided that the police station or the registrar in the Court shall maintain a register to enter the address, email address, phone number and such other details as the State Government may, by rules, provide.

The summons shall, if practicable, be served personally on the person summoned, by delivering or tendering to him one of the duplicates of the summons: Provided that summons bearing the image of Court's seal may also be served by electronic communication in such form and in such manner, as the State Government may, by rules, provide.
Every person on whom a summons is so served personally shall, if so required by the serving officer, sign a receipt therefor on the back of the other duplicate.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Service of summons on corporate bodies, firms, and societies.
content: (1) Service of a summons on a company or corporation may be effected by serving it on the Director, Manager, Secretary or other officer of the company or corporation, or by letter sent by registered post addressed to the Director, Manager, Secretary or other officer of the company or corporation in India, in which case the service shall be deemed to have been effected when the letter would arrive in ordinary course of post. Explanation.In this section, “company” means a body corporate and “corporation” means an incorporated company or other body corporate registered under the Companies Act, 2013 (18 of 2013) or a society registered under the Societies Registration Act, 1860 (21 of 1860). (2) Service of a summons on a firm or other association of individuals may be effected by serving it on any partner of such firm or association, or by letter sent by registered post addressed to such partner, in which case the service shall be deemed to have been effected when the letter would arrive in ordinary course of post.
summary:
If a company or corporation in India receives a court summons, it can be served to any of the following people: 
- A Director
- A Manager
- A Secretary
- Any other officer of the company

Alternatively, the summons can be sent by registered post to any of these people, and it will be considered as served when the letter would normally arrive in the mail.

Similarly, if a firm or group of people receives a court summons, it can be served to any of the partners in the firm. The summons can also be sent by registered post to any partner, and it will be considered as served when the letter would normally arrive in the mail.

Note: This explanation applies to companies registered under the Companies Act, 2013, and societies registered under the Societies Registration Act, 1860.

subsections.text: Service of a summons on a company or corporation may be effected by serving it on the Director, Manager, Secretary or other officer of the company or corporation, or by letter sent by registered post addressed to the Director, Manager, Secretary or other officer of the company or corporation in India, in which case the service shall be deemed to have been effected when the letter would arrive in ordinary course of post.

In this section, “company” means a body corporate and “corporation” means an incorporated company or other body corporate registered under the Companies Act, 2013 (18 of 2013) or a society registered under the Societies Registration Act, 1860 (21 of 1860).

Service of a summons on a firm or other association of individuals may be effected by serving it on any partner of such firm or association, or by letter sent by registered post addressed to such partner, in which case the service shall be deemed to have been effected when the letter would arrive in ordinary course of post.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Service when persons summoned cannot be found.
content: Where the person summoned cannot, by the exercise of due diligence, be found, the summons may be served by leaving one of the duplicates for him with some adult member of his family residing with him, and the person with whom the summons is so left shall, if so required by the serving officer, sign a receipt therefor on the back of the other duplicate. Explanation.A servant is not a member of the family within the meaning of this section.
summary: If someone you need to serve a court summons to cannot be found, you can leave a copy with an adult family member who lives with them. This person must be a family member, not just anyone who works for them, like a servant. If you leave the copy, the person who received it must sign a receipt to confirm it was given to them.

subsections.text: Where the person summoned cannot, by the exercise of due diligence, be found, the summons may be served by leaving one of the duplicates for him with some adult member of his family residing with him, and the person with whom the summons is so left shall, if so required by the serving officer, sign a receipt therefor on the back of the other duplicate.
A servant is not a member of the family within the meaning of this section.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Procedure when service cannot be effected as before provided.
content: If service cannot by the exercise of due diligence be effected as provided in section 64, section 65 or section 66, the serving officer shall affix one of the duplicates of the summons to some conspicuous part of the house or homestead in which the person summoned ordinarily resides; and thereupon the Court, after making such inquiries as it thinks fit, may either declare that the summons has been duly served or order fresh service in such manner as it considers proper.
summary: If a court official tries to serve a summons to someone but can't do it in the usual way, they will post a copy of the summons in a visible place at the person's home. The court will then decide if this was a proper way to serve the summons or if they need to try again.

subsections.text: If service cannot by the exercise of due diligence be effected as provided in section 64, section 65 or section 66, the serving officer shall affix one of the duplicates of the summons to some conspicuous part of the house or homestead in which the person summoned ordinarily resides; and thereupon the Court, after making such inquiries as it thinks fit, may either declare that the summons has been duly served or order fresh service in such manner as it considers proper.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Service on Government servant.
content: (1) Where the person summoned is in the active service of the Government, the Court issuing the summons shall ordinarily send it in duplicate to the head of the office in which such person is employed; and such head shall thereupon cause the summons to be served in the manner provided by section 64, and shall return it to the Court under his signature with the endorsement required by that section. (2) Such signature shall be evidence of due service.
summary: When a government employee is summoned to court, the court sends a copy of the summons to their office head. The office head then makes sure the employee receives the summons, following the rules mentioned in another section (64). After serving the summons, the office head signs it and returns it to the court, confirming that the employee was properly served. This signed document serves as proof that the employee received the summons.

subsections.text: Where the person summoned is in the active service of the Government, the Court issuing the summons shall ordinarily send it in duplicate to the head of the office in which such person is employed; and such head shall thereupon cause the summons to be served in the manner provided by section 64, and shall return it to the Court under his signature with the endorsement required by that section.
Such signature shall be evidence of due service.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Service of summons outside local limits.
content: When a Court desires that a summons issued by it shall be served at any place outside its local jurisdiction, it shall ordinarily send such summons in duplicate to a Magistrate within whose local jurisdiction the person summoned resides, or is, to be there served.
summary: When a court in India wants to serve someone with a court notice or summons who lives outside the area where the court is located, they usually send the summons to a local magistrate in the area where the person lives. The magistrate then serves the summons to the person.

subsections.text: When a Court desires that a summons issued by it shall be served at any place outside its local jurisdiction, it shall ordinarily send such summons in duplicate to a Magistrate within whose local jurisdiction the person summoned resides, or is, to be there served.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Proof of service in such cases and when serving officer not present.
content: (1) When a summons issued by a Court is served outside its local jurisdiction, and in any case where the officer who has served a summons is not present at the hearing of the case, an affidavit, purporting to be made before a Magistrate, that such summons has been served, and a duplicate of the summons purporting to be endorsed (in the manner provided by section 64 or section 66) by the person to whom it was delivered or tendered or with whom it was left, shall be admissible in evidence, and the statements made therein shall be deemed to be correct unless and until the contrary is proved. 36 (2) The affidavit mentioned in this section may be attached to the duplicate of the summons and returned to the Court. (3) All summons served through electronic communication under sections 64 to 71 (both inclusive) shall be considered as duly served and a copy of such summons shall be attested and kept as a proof of service of summons.
summary:
When a court summons is delivered outside its local area or the officer who served it can't attend the court hearing, the person who served it can create an affidavit (a written statement sworn under oath) in front of a magistrate. This affidavit, along with a copy of the summons, will be accepted as proof that the summons was served. 

If the officer who served the summons can't attend the court hearing, they can create this affidavit and attach it to a copy of the summons. This copy will then be returned to the court as evidence.

Additionally, if a summons is sent through electronic communication, it will be considered as having been served properly. A copy of the summons will be kept as proof of service.

subsections.text: When a summons issued by a Court is served outside its local jurisdiction, and in any case where the officer who has served a summons is not present at the hearing of the case, an affidavit, purporting to be made before a Magistrate, that such summons has been served, and a duplicate of the summons purporting to be endorsed (in the manner provided by section 64 or section 66) by the person to whom it was delivered or tendered or with whom it was left, shall be admissible in evidence, and the statements made therein shall be deemed to be correct unless and until the contrary is proved.
The affidavit mentioned in this section may be attached to the duplicate of the summons and returned to the Court.

All summons served through electronic communication under sections 64 to 71 (both inclusive) shall be considered as duly served and a copy of such summons shall be attested and kept as a proof of service of summons.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Service of summons on witness.
content: (1) Notwithstanding anything contained in the preceding sections of this Chapter, a Court issuing a summons to a witness may, in addition to and simultaneously with the issue of such summons, direct a copy of the summons to be served by electronic communication or by registered post addressed to the witness at the place where he ordinarily resides or carries on business or personally works for gain. (2) When an acknowledgement purporting to be signed by the witness or an endorsement purporting to be made by a postal employee that the witness refused to take delivery of the summons has been received or on the proof of delivery of summons under sub-section (3) of section 70 by electronic communication to the satisfaction of the Court, the Court issuing summons may deem that the summons has been duly served. B.Warrant of arrest
summary:
When a court sends a witness a summons, it can also send a copy of the summons by email or by registered post to the witness's usual address. 

To prove that the witness received the summons, the court can accept an acknowledgement signed by the witness or an endorsement by a postal employee saying the witness refused to take delivery. Alternatively, if the summons was sent by email, the court can accept proof of delivery to the witness's email address. 

This means the court can consider the summons as having been delivered to the witness even if they didn't personally receive it.

subsections.text: Notwithstanding anything contained in the preceding sections of this Chapter, a Court issuing a summons to a witness may, in addition to and simultaneously with the issue of such summons, direct a copy of the summons to be served by electronic communication or by registered post addressed to the witness at the place where he ordinarily resides or carries on business or personally works for gain.

When an acknowledgement purporting to be signed by the witness or an endorsement purporting to be made by a postal employee that the witness refused to take delivery of the summons has been received or on the proof of delivery of summons under sub-section (3) of section 70 by electronic communication to the satisfaction of the Court, the Court issuing summons may deem that the summons has been duly served.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Form of warrant of arrest and duration.
content: (1) Every warrant of arrest issued by a Court under this Sanhita shall be in writing, signed by the presiding officer of such Court and shall bear the seal of the Court. (2) Every such warrant shall remain in force until it is cancelled by the Court which issued it, or until it is executed.
summary:
When a court issues an arrest warrant under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, it must be in writing and signed by the judge in charge. The court's official seal should also be on the warrant. 

This warrant remains valid until the court that issued it cancels it or the person is arrested and the process is completed.

subsections.text: Every warrant of arrest issued by a Court under this Sanhita shall be in writing, signed by the presiding officer of such Court and shall bear the seal of the Court.
Every such warrant shall remain in force until it is cancelled by the Court which issued it, or until it is executed.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Power to direct security to be taken.
content: (1) Any Court issuing a warrant for the arrest of any person may in its discretion direct by endorsement on the warrant that, if such person executes a bail bond with sufficient sureties for his attendance before the Court at a specified time and thereafter until otherwise directed by the Court, the officer to whom the warrant is directed shall take such security and shall release such person from custody. (2) The endorsement shall state (a) the number of sureties; (b) the amount in which they and the person for whose arrest the warrant is issued, are to be respectively bound; (c) the time at which he is to attend before the Court. (3) Whenever security is taken under this section, the officer to whom the warrant is directed shall forward the bond to the Court.
summary:
When a court issues an arrest warrant, it has the power to decide if the person can be released on bail instead of being taken into custody immediately. If the court decides to release the person on bail, it will add some conditions to the warrant. 

The court will specify:

- The number of people who must act as sureties (guarantors) for the person who was arrested.
- The amount of money that the person who was arrested and the sureties must promise to pay if the person fails to attend court at the specified time.
- The exact time when the person must attend court.

Once the bail conditions are agreed upon, the officer who received the arrest warrant will take the bail bond from the person and the sureties, and then release the person from custody. The bail bond will then be sent to the court for their records.

subsections.text: Any Court issuing a warrant for the arrest of any person may in its discretion direct by endorsement on the warrant that, if such person executes a bail bond with sufficient sureties for his attendance before the Court at a specified time and thereafter until otherwise directed by the Court, the officer to whom the warrant is directed shall take such security and shall release such person from custody.

The endorsement shall state (a) the number of sureties; (b) the amount in which they and the person for whose arrest the warrant is issued, are to be respectively bound; (c) the time at which he is to attend before the Court.
Whenever security is taken under this section, the officer to whom the warrant is directed shall forward the bond to the Court.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Warrants to whom directed.
content: (1) A warrant of arrest shall ordinarily be directed to one or more police officers; but the Court issuing such a warrant may, if its immediate execution is necessary and no police officer is immediately available, direct it to any other person or persons, and such person or persons shall execute the same. (2) When a warrant is directed to more officers or persons than one, it may be executed by all, or by any one or more of them.
summary:
When a court issues an arrest warrant, it is usually given to one or more police officers. However, if a police officer is not immediately available and the situation requires immediate action, the court can give the warrant to anyone else who can help, such as a security guard or a government official. 

If the warrant is given to multiple people, any one of them can execute it, not just all of them. This means if there are multiple people with the warrant, they can work together or individually to carry out the arrest.

subsections.text: A warrant of arrest shall ordinarily be directed to one or more police officers; but the Court issuing such a warrant may, if its immediate execution is necessary and no police officer is immediately available, direct it to any other person or persons, and such person or persons shall execute the same.
When a warrant is directed to more officers or persons than one, it may be executed by all, or by any one or more of them.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Warrant may be directed to any person.
content: (1) The Chief Judicial Magistrate or a Magistrate of the first class may direct a warrant to any person within his local jurisdiction for the arrest of any escaped convict, proclaimed offender or of any person who is accused of a non-bailable offence and is evading arrest. 37 (2) Such person shall acknowledge in writing the receipt of the warrant, and shall execute it if the person for whose arrest it was issued, is in, or enters on, any land or other property under his charge. (3) When the person against whom such warrant is issued is arrested, he shall be made over with the warrant to the nearest police officer, who shall cause him to be taken before a Magistrate having jurisdiction in the case, unless security is taken under section 73.
summary:
If a person is accused of a serious crime and is hiding, the Chief Judicial Magistrate or a higher-ranking Magistrate can issue a warrant to anyone in their area to arrest this person. 

This person who receives the warrant must acknowledge in writing that they got it and then carry out the arrest if the accused person is found on any property they are in charge of. 

Once the accused person is arrested, they will be handed over to the nearest police officer, who will take them to a Magistrate with the authority to handle the case, unless the accused person is able to post bail.

subsections.text: The Chief Judicial Magistrate or a Magistrate of the first class may direct a warrant to any person within his local jurisdiction for the arrest of any escaped convict, proclaimed offender or of any person who is accused of a non-bailable offence and is evading arrest.

Such person shall acknowledge in writing the receipt of the warrant, and shall execute it if the person for whose arrest it was issued, is in, or enters on, any land or other property under his charge.

When the person against whom such warrant is issued is arrested, he shall be made over with the warrant to the nearest police officer, who shall cause him to be taken before a Magistrate having jurisdiction in the case, unless security is taken under section 73.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Warrant directed to police officer.
content: A warrant directed to any police officer may also be executed by any other police officer whose name is endorsed upon the warrant by the officer to whom it is directed or endorsed.
summary: If a police officer is given a warrant, they can also hand it over to another police officer. This other officer can then carry out the tasks mentioned in the warrant. The original officer's name is usually written on the warrant, and they can also write the name of the officer who will be carrying out the tasks.

subsections.text: A warrant directed to any police officer may also be executed by any other police officer whose name is endorsed upon the warrant by the officer to whom it is directed or endorsed.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Notification of substance of warrant.
content: The police officer or other person executing a warrant of arrest shall notify the substance thereof to the person to be arrested, and, if so required, shall show him the warrant.
summary: When the police come to arrest someone, they must tell the person why they are being arrested and show them the arrest warrant if asked. This is to ensure the person knows why they are being taken into custody and to prevent any confusion or misunderstanding.

subsections.text: The police officer or other person executing a warrant of arrest shall notify the substance thereof to the person to be arrested, and, if so required, shall show him the warrant.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Person arrested to be brought before Court without delay.
content: The police officer or other person executing a warrant of arrest shall (subject to the provisions of section 73 as to security) without unnecessary delay bring the person arrested before the Court before which he is required by law to produce such person: Provided that such delay shall not, in any case, exceed twenty-four hours exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Magistrate's Court.
summary: When a person is arrested, the police must take them to court as soon as possible. They cannot keep the person in custody for more than 24 hours, excluding the time it takes to travel from the place of arrest to the court. This means the police must act quickly and not delay the person's court appearance.

subsections.text: The police officer or other person executing a warrant of arrest shall (subject to the provisions of section 73 as to security) without unnecessary delay bring the person arrested before the Court before which he is required by law to produce such person: Provided that such delay shall not, in any case, exceed twenty-four hours exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Magistrate's Court.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Where warrant may be executed.
content: A warrant of arrest may be executed at any place in India.
summary: A warrant of arrest can be executed anywhere in India, meaning the police can arrest someone with a valid warrant at their home, workplace, or any other location within the country.

subsections.text: A warrant of arrest may be executed at any place in India.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Warrant forwarded for execution outside jurisdiction.
content: (1) When a warrant is to be executed outside the local jurisdiction of the Court issuing it, such Court may, instead of directing the warrant to a police officer within its jurisdiction, forward it by post or otherwise to any Executive Magistrate or District Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police within the local limits of whose jurisdiction it is to be executed; and the Executive Magistrate or District Superintendent or Commissioner shall endorse his name thereon, and if practicable, cause it to be executed in the manner hereinbefore provided. (2) The Court issuing a warrant under sub-section (1) shall forward, along with thewarrant, the substance of the information against the person to be arrested together with such documents, if any, as may be sufficient to enable the Court acting under section 83 to decide whether bail should or should not be granted to the person.
summary:
When a court issues a warrant for someone's arrest, but the person is not in the same area as the court, the court can send the warrant to a higher authority like a District Superintendent of Police or a Commissioner of Police in the area where the person is located. 

This higher authority will then sign the warrant and try to execute it by arresting the person. To do this, the court will also send the details of the case against the person and any supporting documents to the higher authority. This way, the higher authority can decide if the person should be granted bail or not.

subsections.text: When a warrant is to be executed outside the local jurisdiction of the Court issuing it, such Court may, instead of directing the warrant to a police officer within its jurisdiction, forward it by post or otherwise to any Executive Magistrate or District Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police within the local limits of whose jurisdiction it is to be executed; and the Executive Magistrate or District Superintendent or Commissioner shall endorse his name thereon, and if practicable, cause it to be executed in the manner hereinbefore provided.

The Court issuing a warrant under sub-section (1) shall forward, along with the warrant, the substance of the information against the person to be arrested together with such documents, if any, as may be sufficient to enable the Court acting under section 83 to decide whether bail should or should not be granted to the person.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Warrant directed to police officer for execution outside jurisdiction.
content: (1) When a warrant directed to a police officer is to be executed beyond the local jurisdiction of the Court issuing the same, he shall ordinarily take it for endorsement either to an Executive Magistrate or to a police officer not below the rank of an officer in charge of a police station, within the local limits of whose jurisdiction the warrant is to be executed. (2) Such Magistrate or police officer shall endorse his name thereon and such endorsement shall be sufficient authority to the police officer to whom the warrant is directed to execute the same, and the local police shall, if so required, assist such officer in executing such warrant. (3) Whenever there is reason to believe that the delay occasioned by obtaining the endorsement of the Magistrate or police officer within whose local jurisdiction the warrant is to be executed will prevent such execution, the police officer to whom it is directed may execute the same without such endorsement in any place beyond the local jurisdiction of the Court which issued it. 82. Procedure on arrest of person against whom warrant issued.(1) When a warrant of arrest is executed outside the district in which it was issued, the person arrested shall, unless the Court which issued the warrant is within thirty kilometres of the place of arrest or is nearer than the Executive Magistrate or District Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police within the local limits of whose jurisdiction 38 the arrest was made, or unless security is taken under section 73, be taken before such Magistrate or District Superintendent or Commissioner. (2) On the arrest of any person referred to in sub-section (1), the police officer shall forthwith give the information regarding such arrest and the place where the arrested person is being held to the designated police officer in the district and to such officer of another district where the arrested person normally resides.
summary:
When a police officer needs to execute a warrant outside their area of jurisdiction, they must take it to a higher authority like an Executive Magistrate or a senior police officer within the local jurisdiction where the warrant needs to be executed. This higher authority will endorse the warrant, giving the police officer the power to execute it. 

If there's a risk that delaying this process will prevent the execution of the warrant, the police officer can proceed without the endorsement. 

If someone is arrested under a warrant in a different district, they must be taken to a nearby Magistrate, Superintendent of Police, or Commissioner of Police, unless the Court that issued the warrant is nearby or unless security is taken. 

After the arrest, the police officer must inform the designated police officer in the district and the officer in the district where the arrested person normally resides about the arrest and where the person is being held.

subsections.text: When a warrant directed to a police officer is to be executed beyond the local jurisdiction of the Court issuing the same, he shall ordinarily take it for endorsement either to an Executive Magistrate or to a police officer not below the rank of an officer in charge of a police station, within the local limits of whose jurisdiction the warrant is to be executed.

Such Magistrate or police officer shall endorse his name thereon and such endorsement shall be sufficient authority to the police officer to whom the warrant is directed to execute the same, and the local police shall, if so required, assist such officer in executing such warrant.

Whenever there is reason to believe that the delay occasioned by obtaining the endorsement of the Magistrate or police officer within whose local jurisdiction the warrant is to be executed will prevent such execution, the police officer to whom it is directed may execute the same without such endorsement in any place beyond the local jurisdiction of the Court which issued it.
Procedure on arrest of person against whom warrant issued.

When a warrant of arrest is executed outside the district in which it was issued, the person arrested shall, unless the Court which issued the warrant is within thirty kilometres of the place of arrest or is nearer than the Executive Magistrate or District Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police within the local limits of whose jurisdiction 38 the arrest was made, or unless security is taken under section 73, be taken before such Magistrate or District Superintendent or Commissioner.

On the arrest of any person referred to in sub-section (1), the police officer shall forthwith give the information regarding such arrest and the place where the arrested person is being held to the designated police officer in the district and to such officer of another district where the arrested person normally resides.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Procedure on arrest of person against whom warrant issued.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Procedure by Magistrate before whom such person arrested is brought.
content: (1) The Executive Magistrate or District Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police shall, if the person arrested appears to be the person intended by the Court which issued the warrant, direct his removal in custody to such Court: Provided that, if the offence is bailable, and such person is ready and willing to give bail bond to the satisfaction of such Magistrate, District Superintendent or Commissioner, or a direction has been endorsed under section 73 on the warrant and such person is ready and willing to give the security required by such direction, the Magistrate, District Superintendent or Commissioner shall take such bail bond or security, as the case may be, and forward the bond, to the Court which issued the warrant: Provided further that if the offence is a non-bailable one, it shall be lawful for the Chief Judicial Magistrate (subject to the provisions of section 480), or the Sessions Judge, of the district in which the arrest is made on consideration of the information and the documents referred to in sub-section (2) of section 80, to release such person on bail. (2) Nothing in this section shall be deemed to prevent a police officer from taking security under section 73. C.Proclamation and attachment
summary:
When a person is arrested and brought before a Magistrate, the Magistrate will check if the arrested person is indeed the one the court wanted to arrest. 

If the arrested person is involved in a bailable offence and is willing to give a bail bond, the Magistrate will accept the bond and send it to the court that issued the warrant. 

However, if the arrested person is involved in a non-bailable offence, the Chief Judicial Magistrate or Sessions Judge can decide to release them on bail after considering the information and documents provided. 

A police officer can also take security from the arrested person under a different section, even if the Magistrate is not involved.

subsections.text: The Executive Magistrate or District Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police shall, if the person arrested appears to be the person intended by the Court which issued the warrant, direct his removal in custody to such Court: Provided that, if the offence is bailable, and such person is ready and willing to give bail bond to the satisfaction of such Magistrate, District Superintendent or Commissioner, or a direction has been endorsed under section 73 on the warrant and such person is ready and willing to give the security required by such direction, the Magistrate, District Superintendent or Commissioner shall take such bail bond or security, as the case may be, and forward the bond, to the Court which issued the warrant: Provided further that if the offence is a non-bailable one, it shall be lawful for the Chief Judicial Magistrate (subject to the provisions of section 480), or the Sessions Judge, of the district in which the arrest is made on consideration of the information and the documents referred to in sub-section (2) of section 80, to release such person on bail.
Nothing in this section shall be deemed to prevent a police officer from taking security under section 73.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Proclamation for person absconding.
content: (1) If any Court has reason to believe (whether after taking evidence or not) that any person against whom a warrant has been issued by it has absconded or is concealing himself so that such warrant cannot be executed, such Court may publish a written proclamation requiring him to appear at a specified place and at a specified time not less than thirty days from the date of publishing such proclamation. (2) The proclamation shall be published as follows: (i) (a) it shall be publicly read in some conspicuous place of the town or village in which such person ordinarily resides; (b) it shall be affixed to some conspicuous part of the house or homestead in which such person ordinarily resides or to some conspicuous place of such town or village; (c) a copy thereof shall be affixed to some conspicuous part of the Court-house; (ii) the Court may also, if it thinks fit, direct a copy of the proclamation to be published in a daily newspaper circulating in the place in which such person ordinarily resides. (3) A statement in writing by the Court issuing the proclamation to the effect that the proclamation was duly published on a specified day, in the manner specified in clause (i) of sub-section (2), shall be conclusive evidence that the requirements of this section have been complied with, and that the proclamation was published on such day. (4) Where a proclamation published under sub-section (1) is in respect of a person accused of an offence which is made punishable with imprisonment of ten years or more, or imprisonment for life or with death under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 or under any other law for the time being in force, and such person fails to appear at the specified place and time required by the proclamation, the Court may, after making such inquiry as it thinks fit, pronounce him a proclaimed offender and make a declaration to that effect. 39 (5) The provisions of sub-sections (2) and (3) shall apply to a declaration made by the Court under sub-section (4) as they apply to the proclamation published under sub-section (1).
summary:
If a court thinks someone has avoided arrest by hiding, it can issue a public notice asking them to appear at a specific place and time. 

To do this, the court will:

- Read the notice out loud in a public place where the person usually lives.
- Post the notice on the person's home or in a public area near their home.
- Post a copy of the notice at the court building.
- Optionally, the court can also publish the notice in a local newspaper.

The court can prove that it followed these steps by providing a written statement. 

If the person is accused of a serious crime and doesn't show up as required, the court can declare them a "proclaimed offender" after investigating. This means the person is considered guilty of avoiding arrest and can face further consequences.

subsections.text: If any Court has reason to believe (whether after taking evidence or not) that any person against whom a warrant has been issued by it has absconded or is concealing himself so that such warrant cannot be executed, such Court may publish a written proclamation requiring him to appear at a specified place and at a specified time not less than thirty days from the date of publishing such proclamation.
The proclamation shall be published as follows:
it shall be publicly read in some conspicuous place of the town or village in which such person ordinarily resides;
it shall be affixed to some conspicuous part of the house or homestead in which such person ordinarily resides or to some conspicuous place of such town or village;
a copy thereof shall be affixed to some conspicuous part of the Court-house;
the Court may also, if it thinks fit, direct a copy of the proclamation to be published in a daily newspaper circulating in the place in which such person ordinarily resides.

A statement in writing by the Court issuing the proclamation to the effect that the proclamation was duly published on a specified day, in the manner specified in clause (i) of sub-section (2), shall be conclusive evidence that the requirements of this section have been complied with, and that the proclamation was published on such day.

Where a proclamation published under sub-section (1) is in respect of a person accused of an offence which is made punishable with imprisonment of ten years or more, or imprisonment for life or with death under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 or under any other law for the time being in force, and such person fails to appear at the specified place and time required by the proclamation, the Court may, after making such inquiry as it thinks fit, pronounce him a proclaimed offender and make a declaration to that effect.
The provisions of sub-sections (2) and (3) shall apply to a declaration made by the Court under sub-section (4) as they apply to the proclamation published under sub-section (1).

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Attachment of property of person absconding.
content: (1) The Court issuing a proclamation under section 84 may, for reasons to be recorded in writing, at any time after the issue of the proclamation, order the attachment of any property, movable or immovable, or both, belonging to the proclaimed person: Provided that where at the time of the issue of the proclamation the Court is satisfied, by affidavit or otherwise, that the person in relation to whom the proclamation is to be issued, (a) is about to dispose of the whole or any part of his property; or (b) is about to remove the whole or any part of his property from the local jurisdiction of the Court, it may order the attachment of property simultaneously with the issue of the proclamation. (2) Such order shall authorise the attachment of any property belonging to such person within the district in which it is made; and it shall authorise the attachment of any property belonging to such person without such district when endorsed by the District Magistrate within whose district such property is situate. (3) If the property ordered to be attached is a debt or other movable property, the attachment under this section shall be made (a) by seizure; or (b) by the appointment of a receiver; or (c) by an order in writing prohibiting the delivery of such property to the proclaimed person or to any one on his behalf; or (d) by all or any two of such methods, as the Court thinks fit. (4) If the property ordered to be attached is immovable, the attachment under this section shall, in the case of land paying revenue to the State Government, be made through the Collector of the district in which the land is situate, and in all other cases (a) by taking possession; or (b) by the appointment of a receiver; or (c) by an order in writing prohibiting the payment of rent on delivery of property to the proclaimed person or to any one on his behalf; or (d) by all or any two of such methods, as the Court thinks fit. (5) If the property ordered to be attached consists of live-stock or is of a perishable nature, the Court may, if it thinks it expedient, order immediate sale thereof, and in such case the proceeds of the sale shall abide the order of the Court. (6) The powers, duties and liabilities of a receiver appointed under this section shall be the same as those of a receiver appointed under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908).
summary:
When a person is declared absconding, the court can order the attachment of their property. This means the court can freeze or seize their assets to prevent them from being sold or moved.

If the court thinks the person is about to sell or move their property, they can order attachment at the same time as issuing the proclamation. 

The attachment order allows the authorities to seize or freeze property within the district where the order is made, and in other districts with the permission of the District Magistrate.

The court can use different methods to attach property, such as:

- Seizing movable property like cash, goods, or vehicles.
- Appointing a receiver to manage the property.
- Prohibiting the delivery of the property to the absconding person or their representatives.
- Selling perishable items like livestock immediately.

For immovable property like land, the attachment is made through the Collector in the district where the land is located, or by taking possession, appointing a receiver, or prohibiting rent payments.

In some cases, the court can order the immediate sale of perishable items like livestock, and the proceeds will be held until the court decides what to do with them.

A receiver appointed under this section has the same powers, duties, and liabilities as a receiver appointed under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

subsections.text: The Court issuing a proclamation under section 84 may, for reasons to be recorded in writing, at any time after the issue of the proclamation, order the attachment of any property, movable or immovable, or both, belonging to the proclaimed person: Provided that where at the time of the issue of the proclamation the Court is satisfied, by affidavit or otherwise, that the person in relation to whom the proclamation is to be issued, (a) is about to dispose of the whole or any part of his property; or (b) is about to remove the whole or any part of his property from the local jurisdiction of the Court, it may order the attachment of property simultaneously with the issue of the proclamation.

Such order shall authorise the attachment of any property belonging to such person within the district in which it is made; and it shall authorise the attachment of any property belonging to such person without such district when endorsed by the District Magistrate within whose district such property is situate.
If the property ordered to be attached is a debt or other movable property, the attachment under this section shall be made (a) by seizure;
or (b) by the appointment of a receiver;
or (c) by an order in writing prohibiting the delivery of such property to the proclaimed person or to any one on his behalf;
or (d) by all or any two of such methods, as the Court thinks fit.

If the property ordered to be attached is immovable, the attachment under this section shall, in the case of land paying revenue to the State Government, be made through the Collector of the district in which the land is situate, and in all other cases
(a) by taking possession;
or (b) by the appointment of a receiver;
or (c) by an order in writing prohibiting the payment of rent on delivery of property to the proclaimed person or to any one on his behalf;
or (d) by all or any two of such methods, as the Court thinks fit.

If the property ordered to be attached consists of live-stock or is of a perishable nature, the Court may, if it thinks it expedient, order immediate sale thereof, and in such case the proceeds of the sale shall abide the order of the Court.
The powers, duties and liabilities of a receiver appointed under this section shall be the same as those of a receiver appointed under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908).

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Identification and attachment of property of proclaimed person.
content: The Court may, on the written request from a police officer not below the rank of the Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police, initiate the process of requesting assistance from a Court or an authority in the contracting State for identification, attachment and forfeiture of property belonging to a proclaimed person in accordance with the procedure provided in Chapter VIII.
summary: When a person is declared a proclaimed offender by the court, the police can ask the court to help identify and freeze the person's properties. This can be done by sending a written request to the court from a senior police officer, such as a Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police. The court will then ask for help from another country's court or authority if the property is located abroad, following the procedures outlined in Chapter VIII of the law.

subsections.text: The Court may, on the written request from a police officer not below the rank of the Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police, initiate the process of requesting assistance from a Court or an authority in the contracting State for identification, attachment and forfeiture of property belonging to a proclaimed person in accordance with the procedure provided in Chapter VIII.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Claims and objections to attachment.
content: (1) If any claim is preferred to, or objection made to the attachment of, any property attached under section 85, within six months from the date of such attachment, by any person other than the proclaimed person, on the ground that the claimant or objector has an interest 40 in such property, and that such interest is not liable to attachment under section 85, the claim or objection shall be inquired into, and may be allowed or disallowed in whole or in part: Provided that any claim preferred or objection made within the period allowed by this sub-section may, in the event of the death of the claimant or objector, be continued by his legal representative. (2) Claims or objections under sub-section (1) may be preferred or made in the Court by which the order of attachment is issued, or, if the claim or objection is in respect of property attached under an order endorsed under sub-section (2) of section 85, in the Court of the Chief Judicial Magistrate of the district in which the attachment is made. (3) Every such claim or objection shall be inquired into by the Court in which it is preferred or made: Provided that, if it is preferred or made in the Court of a Chief Judicial Magistrate, he may make it over for disposal to any Magistrate subordinate to him. (4) Any person whose claim or objection has been disallowed in whole or in part by an order under sub-section (1) may, within a period of one year from the date of such order, institute a suit to establish the right which he claims in respect of the property in dispute; but subject to the result of such suit, if any, the order shall be conclusive.
summary:
If someone thinks their property is being wrongly attached, they can file a claim or objection within six months. 

This claim or objection must be made in writing and in a specific court, which is either the court that issued the attachment order or the court of the Chief Judicial Magistrate of the district where the attachment was made.

The court will then investigate the claim or objection. If the claimant or objector dies, their legal representative can continue the claim or objection on their behalf.

If the court disallows the claim or objection, the person can still file a lawsuit within one year to prove their right to the property. The court's initial decision will be final unless the person wins their lawsuit, in which case the court's decision will be changed.

subsections.text: If any claim is preferred to, or objection made to the attachment of, any property attached under section 85, within six months from the date of such attachment, by any person other than the proclaimed person, on the ground that the claimant or objector has an interest 40 in such property, and that such interest is not liable to attachment under section 85, the claim or objection shall be inquired into, and may be allowed or disallowed in whole or in part: Provided that any claim preferred or objection made within the period allowed by this sub-section may, in the event of the death of the claimant or objector, be continued by his legal representative.

Claims or objections under sub-section (1) may be preferred or made in the Court by which the order of attachment is issued, or, if the claim or objection is in respect of property attached under an order endorsed under sub-section (2) of section 85, in the Court of the Chief Judicial Magistrate of the district in which the attachment is made.

Every such claim or objection shall be inquired into by the Court in which it is preferred or made: Provided that, if it is preferred or made in the Court of a Chief Judicial Magistrate, he may make it over for disposal to any Magistrate subordinate to him.

Any person whose claim or objection has been disallowed in whole or in part by an order under sub-section (1) may, within a period of one year from the date of such order, institute a suit to establish the right which he claims in respect of the property in dispute; but subject to the result of such suit, if any, the order shall be conclusive.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Release, sale and restoration of attached property.
content: (1) If the proclaimed person appears within the time specified in the proclamation, the Court shall make an order releasing the property from the attachment. (2) If the proclaimed person does not appear within the time specified in the proclamation, the property under the attachment shall be at the disposal of the State Government; but it shall not be sold until the expiration of six months from the date of the attachment and until any claim preferred or objection made under section 87 has been disposed of under that section, unless it is subject to speedy and natural decay, or the Court considers that the sale would be for the benefit of the owner; in either of which cases the Court may cause it to be sold whenever it thinks fit. (3) If, within two years from the date of the attachment, any person whose property is or has been at the disposal of the State Government under sub-section (2), appears voluntarily or is apprehended and brought before the Court by whose order the property was attached, or the Court to which such Court is subordinate, and proves to the satisfaction of such Court that he did not abscond or conceal himself for the purpose of avoiding execution of the warrant, and that he had not such notice of the proclamation as to enable him to attend within the time specified therein, such property, or, if the same has been sold, the net proceeds of the sale, or, if part only thereof has been sold, the net proceeds of the sale and the residue of the property, shall, after satisfying therefrom all costs incurred in consequence of the attachment, be delivered to him.
summary:
If a person whose property is attached appears within the time specified in the proclamation, the court will release the property from attachment. 

If the person does not appear, the government will take control of the property. However, the property cannot be sold for six months or until any claims or objections are resolved. If the property is perishable or selling it would benefit the owner, the court can sell it at any time.

If the person whose property is attached appears within two years, proves they didn't intentionally avoid the attachment, and didn't know about the proclamation in time, they can get their property back. If the property has been sold, they will receive the net proceeds of the sale.

subsections.text: If the proclaimed person appears within the time specified in the proclamation, the Court shall make an order releasing the property from the attachment.

If the proclaimed person does not appear within the time specified in the proclamation, the property under the attachment shall be at the disposal of the State Government; but it shall not be sold until the expiration of six months from the date of the attachment and until any claim preferred or objection made under section 87 has been disposed of under that section, unless it is subject to speedy and natural decay, or the Court considers that the sale would be for the benefit of the owner; in either of which cases the Court may cause it to be sold whenever it thinks fit.

If, within two years from the date of the attachment, any person whose property is or has been at the disposal of the State Government under sub-section (2), appears voluntarily or is apprehended and brought before the Court by whose order the property was attached, or the Court to which such Court is subordinate, and proves to the satisfaction of such Court that he did not abscond or conceal himself for the purpose of avoiding execution of the warrant, and that he had not such notice of the proclamation as to enable him to attend within the time specified therein, such property, or, if the same has been sold, the net proceeds of the sale, or, if part only thereof has been sold, the net proceeds of the sale and the residue of the property, shall, after satisfying therefrom all costs incurred in consequence of the attachment, be delivered to him.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Appeal from order rejecting application for restoration of attached property.
content: Any person referred to in sub-section (3) of section 88, who is aggrieved by any refusal to deliver property or the proceeds of the sale thereof may appeal to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from the sentences of the first-mentioned Court. D.Other rules regarding processes
summary: If the government has taken your property and you've applied to get it back, but they've refused, you can appeal to a higher court to review their decision. This appeal should be made to the court that usually hears appeals from the court that initially made the decision.

subsections.text: Any person referred to in sub-section (3) of section 88, who is aggrieved by any refusal to deliver property or the proceeds of the sale thereof may appeal to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from the sentences of the first-mentioned Court.
Other rules regarding processes

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Issue of warrant in lieu of, or in addition to, summons.
content: A Court may, in any case in which it is empowered by this Sanhita to issue a summons for the appearance of any person, issue, after recording its reasons in writing, a warrant for his arrest (a) if, either before the issue of such summons, or after the issue of the same but before the time fixed for his appearance, the Court sees reason to believe that he has absconded or will not obey the summons; or 41 (b) if at such time he fails to appear and the summons is proved to have been duly served in time to admit of his appearing in accordance therewith and no reasonable excuse is offered for such failure.
summary:
If a court needs someone to appear in court, it can issue a warrant for their arrest instead of or in addition to a summons. This can happen in two situations: 

1. If the court thinks the person has run away or won't show up even after being asked to appear in court.
2. If the person doesn't show up to court despite being served the summons on time and they don't have a valid reason for not attending. 

Before issuing the warrant, the court must write down its reasons for doing so.

subsections.text: A Court may, in any case in which it is empowered by this Sanhita to issue a summons for the appearance of any person, issue, after recording its reasons in writing, a warrant for his arrest

if, either before the issue of such summons, or after the issue of the same but before the time fixed for his appearance, the Court sees reason to believe that he has absconded or will not obey the summons;
if at such time he fails to appear and the summons is proved to have been duly served in time to admit of his appearing in accordance therewith and no reasonable excuse is offered for such failure.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Power to take bond or bail bond for appearance.
content: When any person for whose appearance or arrest the officer presiding in any Court is empowered to issue a summons or warrant, is present in such Court, such officer may require such person to execute a bond or bail bond for his appearance in such Court, or any other Court to which the case may be transferred for trial.
summary: When a court orders someone to appear, the officer in charge can ask that person to sign a bond or a bail bond. This means the person promises to show up in court on the required date. If the case is transferred to another court, the person still has to appear there. This bond or bail bond is like a promise to the court that the person will not skip their court dates.

subsections.text: When any person for whose appearance or arrest the officer presiding in any Court is empowered to issue a summons or warrant, is present in such Court, such officer may require such person to execute a bond or bail bond for his appearance in such Court, or any other Court to which the case may be transferred for trial.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Arrest on breach of bond or bail bond for appearance.
content: When any person who is bound by any bond or bail bond taken under this Sanhita to appear before a Court, does not appear, the officer presiding in such Court may issue a warrant directing that such person be arrested and produced before him.
summary: If someone has given a promise or a bond to appear in court, but they fail to do so, the court can issue a warrant for their arrest. This means the police will have to catch and bring this person to court to face the consequences of not keeping their promise.

subsections.text: When any person who is bound by any bond or bail bond taken under this Sanhita to appear before a Court, does not appear, the officer presiding in such Court may issue a warrant directing that such person be arrested and produced before him.

chapter: CHAPTER VI: PROCESSES TO COMPEL APPEARANCE
title: Provisions of this Chapter generally applicable to summons and warrants of arrest.
content: The provisions contained in this Chapter relating to summons and warrant, and their issue, service and execution, shall, so far as may be, apply to every summons and every warrant of arrest issued under this Sanhita. CHAPTER VII PROCESSES TO COMPEL THE PRODUCTION OF THINGS A.Summons to produce
summary: This section explains that the rules and procedures in this chapter about summons and arrest warrants will apply to all such documents issued under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS). In simpler terms, the procedures for issuing, serving, and enforcing summons and arrest warrants will be the same for all cases under this law.

subsections.text: The provisions contained in this Chapter relating to summons and warrant, and their issue, service and execution, shall, so far as may be, apply to every summons and every warrant of arrest issued under this Sanhita.
CHAPTER VII PROCESSES TO COMPEL THE PRODUCTION OF THINGS
A.Summons to produce

chapter: CHAPTER VII: PROCESSES TO COMPEL THE PRODUCTION OF THINGS
title: Summons to produce document or other thing.
content: (1) Whenever any Court or any officer in charge of a police station considers that the production of any document, electronic communication, including communication devices, which is likely to contain digital evidence or other thing is necessary or desirable for the purposes of any investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita by or before such Court or officer, such Court may issue a summons or such officer may, by a written order, either in physical form or in electronic form, require the person in whose possession or power such document or thing is believed to be, to attend and produce it, or to produce it, at the time and place stated in the summons or order. (2) Any person required under this section merely to produce a document, or other thing shall be deemed to have complied with the requisition if he causes such document or thing to be produced instead of attending personally to produce the same. (3) Nothing in this section shall be deemed (a) to affect sections 129 and 130 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 or the Bankers’ Books Evidence Act, 1891 (13 of 1891); or (b) to apply to a letter, postcard, or other document or any parcel or thing in the custody of the postal authority.
summary:
When a court or police officer thinks they need a document or other item to help with an investigation or case, they can send a summons to the person who has it. This summons will ask the person to bring the document or item to the court or police station at a specific time and place.

The person who receives the summons doesn't necessarily have to be present in person; they can just arrange for the document or item to be brought instead. 

This law doesn't affect other laws related to evidence and doesn't apply to items like letters, postcards, or packages that are in the custody of the postal service.

subsections.text: Whenever any Court or any officer in charge of a police station considers that the production of any document, electronic communication, including communication devices, which is likely to contain digital evidence or other thing is necessary or desirable for the purposes of any investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita by or before such Court or officer, such Court may issue a summons or such officer may, by a written order, either in physical form or in electronic form, require the person in whose possession or power such document or thing is believed to be, to attend and produce it, or to produce it, at the time and place stated in the summons or order.

Any person required under this section merely to produce a document, or other thing shall be deemed to have complied with the requisition if he causes such document or thing to be produced instead of attending personally to produce the same.
Nothing in this section shall be deemed to affect sections 129 and 130 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 or the Bankers’ Books Evidence Act, 1891 (13 of 1891);
to apply to a letter, postcard, or other document or any parcel or thing in the custody of the postal authority.

chapter: CHAPTER VII: PROCESSES TO COMPEL THE PRODUCTION OF THINGS
title: Procedure as to letters.
content: (1) If any document, parcel or thing in the custody of a postal authority is, in the opinion of the District Magistrate, Chief Judicial Magistrate, Court of Session or High Court wanted for the purpose of any investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, such Magistrate or Court may require the postal authority to deliver the document, parcel or thing to such person as the Magistrate or Court directs.
