Where an appeal presented under section 424 has been dismissed summarily under this section and the Appellate Court finds that another petition of appeal duly presented under section 423 on behalf of the same appellant has not been considered by it, that Court may, notwithstanding anything contained in section 434, if satisfied that it is necessary in the interests of justice so to do, hear and dispose of such appeal in accordance with law.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXI: APPEALS
title: Procedure for hearing appeals not dismissed summarily.
content: (1) If the Appellate Court does not dismiss the appeal summarily, it shall cause notice of the time and place at which such appeal will be heard to be given (i) to the appellant or his advocate; (ii) to such officer as the State Government may appoint in this behalf; (iii) if the appeal is from a judgment of conviction in a case instituted upon complaint, to the complainant; (iv) if the appeal is under section 418 or section 419, to the accused, and shall also furnish such officer, complainant and accused with a copy of the grounds of appeal. (2) The Appellate Court shall then send for the record of the case, if such record is not already available in that Court, and hear the parties: Provided that if the appeal is only as to the extent or the legality of the sentence, the Court may dispose of the appeal without sending for the record. (3) Where the only ground for appeal from a conviction is the alleged severity of the sentence, the appellant shall not, except with the leave of the Court, urge or be heard in support of any other ground.
summary:
If an appeal is not dismissed immediately, the higher court will send a notice to the person who filed the appeal, their lawyer, and other relevant parties, such as the officer who made the complaint or the accused, telling them when and where the appeal will be heard. They will also be given a copy of the reasons why the appeal was filed.

The higher court will then gather the case records and hold a hearing where all parties involved can present their arguments. However, if the appeal is only about the length or legality of the sentence, the court might not need the full case records to make a decision.

If someone appeals a conviction because they think the sentence was too harsh, they can only argue about the sentence and not about any other aspect of the case, unless the court gives them permission to do so.

subsections.text: If the Appellate Court does not dismiss the appeal summarily, it shall cause notice of the time and place at which such appeal will be heard to be given (i) to the appellant or his advocate; (ii) to such officer as the State Government may appoint in this behalf; (iii) if the appeal is from a judgment of conviction in a case instituted upon complaint, to the complainant; (iv) if the appeal is under section 418 or section 419, to the accused, and shall also furnish such officer, complainant and accused with a copy of the grounds of appeal.

The Appellate Court shall then send for the record of the case, if such record is not already available in that Court, and hear the parties: Provided that if the appeal is only as to the extent or the legality of the sentence, the Court may dispose of the appeal without sending for the record.

Where the only ground for appeal from a conviction is the alleged severity of the sentence, the appellant shall not, except with the leave of the Court, urge or be heard in support of any other ground.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXI: APPEALS
title: Powers of Appellate Court.
content: After perusing such record and hearing the appellant or his advocate, if he appears, and the Public Prosecutor if he appears, and in case of an appeal under section 418 or section 419, the accused, if he appears, the Appellate Court may, if it considers that there is no sufficient ground for interfering, dismiss the appeal, or may (a) in an appeal from an order of acquittal, reverse such order and direct that further inquiry be made, or that the accused be re-tried or committed for trial, as the case may be, or find him guilty and pass sentence on him according to law; (b) in an appeal from a conviction (i) reverse the finding and sentence and acquit or discharge the accused, or order him to be re-tried by a Court of competent jurisdiction subordinate to such Appellate Court or committed for trial; or (ii) alter the finding, maintaining the sentence; or (iii) with or without altering the finding, alter the nature or the extent, or the nature and extent, of the sentence, but not so as to enhance the same; (c) in an appeal for enhancement of sentence (i) reverse the finding and sentence and acquit or discharge the accused or order him to be re-tried by a Court competent to try the offence; or (ii) alter the finding maintaining the sentence; or 147 (iii) with or without altering the finding, alter the nature or the extent, or, the nature and extent, of the sentence, so as to enhance or reduce the same; (d) in an appeal from any other order, alter or reverse such order; (e) make any amendment or any consequential or incidental order that may be just or proper: Provided that the sentence shall not be enhanced unless the accused has had an opportunity of showing cause against such enhancement: Provided further that the Appellate Court shall not inflict greater punishment for the offence which in its opinion the accused has committed, than might have been inflicted for that offence by the Court passing the order or sentence under appeal.
summary:
When an appeal is made to a higher court, the court will review the case records and listen to the person who filed the appeal, their lawyer, the public prosecutor, and if the accused is present, they will also be heard. 

The higher court can then make one of the following decisions: 

- If the appeal has no valid grounds, it can be dismissed.
- If the accused was found not guilty, the higher court can order a new investigation or trial, or it can find the accused guilty and give them a sentence.
- If the accused was found guilty, the higher court can:
  - Overturn the guilty verdict and free the accused.
  - Change the verdict but keep the same sentence.
  - Change the sentence, either making it harsher or milder, but not increasing it if the accused hasn't been given a chance to object.
- If the appeal is about increasing the sentence, the higher court can:
  - Overturn the guilty verdict and free the accused.
  - Change the verdict but keep the same sentence.
  - Change the sentence, either making it harsher or milder, and can increase it if the accused has been given a chance to object.
- For any other type of appeal, the higher court can change or reverse the original order.
- The higher court can also make any other necessary changes to the case.

However, the higher court cannot give a harsher sentence than what the original court could have given, unless the accused has been given a chance to object to the increased sentence.

subsections.text: After perusing such record and hearing the appellant or his advocate, if he appears, and the Public Prosecutor if he appears, and in case of an appeal under section 418 or section 419, the accused, if he appears, the Appellate Court may, if it considers that there is no sufficient ground for interfering, dismiss the appeal, or may

in an appeal from an order of acquittal, reverse such order and direct that further inquiry be made, or that the accused be re-tried or committed for trial, as the case may be, or find him guilty and pass sentence on him according to law;
reverse the finding and sentence and acquit or discharge the accused, or order him to be re-tried by a Court of competent jurisdiction subordinate to such Appellate Court or committed for trial;
alter the finding, maintaining the sentence;
with or without altering the finding, alter the nature or the extent, or the nature and extent, of the sentence, but not so as to enhance the same;
reverse the finding and sentence and acquit or discharge the accused or order him to be re-tried by a Court competent to try the offence;
alter the finding maintaining the sentence;
with or without altering the finding, alter the nature or the extent, or, the nature and extent, of the sentence, so as to enhance or reduce the same;
in an appeal from any other order, alter or reverse such order;
make any amendment or any consequential or incidental order that may be just or proper:
Provided that the sentence shall not be enhanced unless the accused has had an opportunity of showing cause against such enhancement:

Provided further that the Appellate Court shall not inflict greater punishment for the offence which in its opinion the accused has committed, than might have been inflicted for that offence by the Court passing the order or sentence under appeal.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXI: APPEALS
title: Judgments of subordinate Appellate Court.
content: The rules contained in Chapter XXIX as to the judgment of a Criminal Court of original jurisdiction shall apply, so far as may be practicable, to the judgment in appeal of a Court of Session or Chief Judicial Magistrate: Provided that, unless the Appellate Court otherwise directs, the accused shall not be brought up, or required to attend, to hear judgment delivered.
summary: When a higher court (like a Court of Session or Chief Judicial Magistrate) reviews a case, it will follow the same rules as the original court that heard the case, as much as possible. However, the court will not require the accused person to attend or be present when the judgment is delivered, unless the court specifically orders them to do so.

subsections.text: The rules contained in Chapter XXIX as to the judgment of a Criminal Court of original jurisdiction shall apply, so far as may be practicable, to the judgment in appeal of a Court of Session or Chief Judicial Magistrate:
Provided that, unless the Appellate Court otherwise directs, the accused shall not be brought up, or required to attend, to hear judgment delivered.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXI: APPEALS
title: Order of High Court on appeal to be certified to lower Court.
content: (1) Whenever a case is decided on appeal by the High Court under this Chapter, it shall certify its judgment or order to the Court by which the finding, sentence or order appealed against was recorded or passed and if such Court is that of a Judicial Magistrate other than the Chief Judicial Magistrate, the High Court's judgment or order shall be sent through the Chief Judicial Magistrate, and if such Court is that of an Executive Magistrate, the High Court's judgment or order shall be sent through the District Magistrate. (2) The Court to which the High Court certifies its judgment or order shall thereupon make such orders as are conformable to the judgment or order of the High Court; and if necessary, the record shall be amended in accordance therewith.
summary: When the High Court makes a decision on an appeal, it sends its judgment or order to the lower court where the original case was heard. If the lower court is run by a Judicial Magistrate (other than the Chief Judicial Magistrate), the High Court sends the judgment through the Chief Judicial Magistrate. If the lower court is run by an Executive Magistrate, the High Court sends the judgment through the District Magistrate. The lower court then makes new orders that match the High Court's decision. If needed, the lower court also updates its records to reflect the High Court's judgment.

subsections.text: Whenever a case is decided on appeal by the High Court under this Chapter, it shall certify its judgment or order to the Court by which the finding, sentence or order appealed against was recorded or passed and if such Court is that of a Judicial Magistrate other than the Chief Judicial Magistrate, the High Court's judgment or order shall be sent through the Chief Judicial Magistrate, and if such Court is that of an Executive Magistrate, the High Court's judgment or order shall be sent through the District Magistrate.

The Court to which the High Court certifies its judgment or order shall thereupon make such orders as are conformable to the judgment or order of the High Court; and if necessary, the record shall be amended in accordance therewith.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXI: APPEALS
title: Suspension of sentence pending appeal; release of appellant on bail.
content: (1) Pending any appeal by a convicted person, the Appellate Court may, for reasons to be recorded by it in writing, order that the execution of the sentence or order appealed against be suspended and, also, if he is in confinement, that he be released on bail, or on his own bond or bail bond: Provided that the Appellate Court shall, before releasing on his own bond or bail bond a convicted person who is convicted of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term of not less than ten years, shall give opportunity to the Public Prosecutor for showing cause in writing against such release: Provided further that in cases where a convicted person is released on bail it shall be open to the Public Prosecutor to file an application for the cancellation of the bail. (2) The power conferred by this section on an Appellate Court may be exercised also by the High Court in the case of an appeal by a convicted person to a Court subordinate thereto. (3) Where the convicted person satisfies the Court by which he is convicted that he intends to present an appeal, the Court shall, (i) where such person, being on bail, is sentenced to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years; or (ii) where the offence of which such person has been convicted is a bailable one, and he is on bail, order that the convicted person be released on bail, unless there are special reasons for refusing bail, for such period as will afford sufficient time to present the appeal and obtain the orders of the Appellate Court 148 under sub-section (1); and the sentence of imprisonment shall, so long as he is so released on bail, be deemed to be suspended. (4) When the appellant is ultimately sentenced to imprisonment for a term or to imprisonment for life, the time during which he is so released shall be excluded in computing the term for which he is so sentenced.
summary:
When a person is found guilty in a court and sentenced, they can appeal the decision to a higher court. Before the appeal is heard, the higher court can decide to suspend the sentence and release the person on bail. 

For this to happen, the higher court must have a good reason to record in writing. If the person was sentenced to death or a long prison term (more than 10 years), the court must give the public prosecutor a chance to explain why the person should not be released on bail.

If the person is released on bail, the public prosecutor can ask the court to cancel the bail. The higher court can also decide to release the person on bail if they are on bail and are sentenced to a short prison term (less than 3 years) or if the crime they committed is a bailable one.

If the person is released on bail, they can still appeal the sentence and the time they spent on bail will not count towards their prison term if they are eventually sentenced to a long prison term.

subsections.text: Pending any appeal by a convicted person, the Appellate Court may, for reasons to be recorded by it in writing, order that the execution of the sentence or order appealed against be suspended and, also, if he is in confinement, that he be released on bail, or on his own bond or bail bond: Provided that the Appellate Court shall, before releasing on his own bond or bail bond a convicted person who is convicted of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term of not less than ten years, shall give opportunity to the Public Prosecutor for showing cause in writing against such release: Provided further that in cases where a convicted person is released on bail it shall be open to the Public Prosecutor to file an application for the cancellation of the bail.
The power conferred by this section on an Appellate Court may be exercised also by the High Court in the case of an appeal by a convicted person to a Court subordinate thereto.

Where the convicted person satisfies the Court by which he is convicted that he intends to present an appeal, the Court shall, (i) where such person, being on bail, is sentenced to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years; or (ii) where the offence of which such person has been convicted is a bailable one, and he is on bail, order that the convicted person be released on bail, unless there are special reasons for refusing bail, for such period as will afford sufficient time to present the appeal and obtain the orders of the Appellate Court 148 under sub-section (1); and the sentence of imprisonment shall, so long as he is so released on bail, be deemed to be suspended.

When the appellant is ultimately sentenced to imprisonment for a term or to imprisonment for life, the time during which he is so released shall be excluded in computing the term for which he is so sentenced.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXI: APPEALS
title: Arrest of accused in appeal from acquittal.
content: When an appeal is presented under section 419, the High Court may issue a warrant directing that the accused be arrested and brought before it or any subordinate Court, and the Court before which he is brought may commit him to prison pending the disposal of the appeal or admit him to bail.
summary: If someone is acquitted (found not guilty) in a lower court, but the prosecution appeals to a higher court (the High Court) and the appeal is accepted, the High Court can order the person to be arrested and brought before them. The High Court can then decide to keep the person in prison until the appeal is resolved or allow them to be released on bail.

subsections.text: When an appeal is presented under section 419, the High Court may issue a warrant directing that the accused be arrested and brought before it or any subordinate Court, and the Court before which he is brought may commit him to prison pending the disposal of the appeal or admit him to bail.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXI: APPEALS
title: Appellate Court may take further evidence or direct it to be taken.
content: (1) In dealing with any appeal under this Chapter, the Appellate Court, if it thinks additional evidence to be necessary, shall record its reasons and may either take such evidence itself, or direct it to be taken by a Magistrate or, when the Appellate Court is a High Court, by a Court of Session or a Magistrate. (2) When the additional evidence is taken by the Court of Session or the Magistrate, it or he shall certify such evidence to the Appellate Court, and such Court shall thereupon proceed to dispose of the appeal. (3) The accused or his advocate shall have the right to be present when the additional evidence is taken. (4) The taking of evidence under this section shall be subject to the provisions of Chapter XXV, as if it were an inquiry.
summary:
When someone appeals a decision in court, the appellate court can decide if they need more evidence to make a fair decision. If they think more evidence is needed, they'll explain why and can either take the evidence themselves or ask a lower court (like a Magistrate) to take it. 

If the lower court takes the evidence, they'll send it to the appellate court, and the appellate court will then make a decision on the appeal. The person who's being accused has the right to be present when the additional evidence is being taken. The process of taking this evidence follows the same rules as an inquiry, which ensures everything is done fairly and properly.

subsections.text: In dealing with any appeal under this Chapter, the Appellate Court, if it thinks additional evidence to be necessary, shall record its reasons and may either take such evidence itself, or direct it to be taken by a Magistrate or, when the Appellate Court is a High Court, by a Court of Session or a Magistrate.

When the additional evidence is taken by the Court of Session or the Magistrate, it or he shall certify such evidence to the Appellate Court, and such Court shall thereupon proceed to dispose of the appeal.
The accused or his advocate shall have the right to be present when the additional evidence is taken.
The taking of evidence under this section shall be subject to the provisions of Chapter XXV, as if it were an inquiry.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXI: APPEALS
title: Procedure where Judges of Court of appeal are equally divided.
content: When an appeal under this Chapter is heard by a High Court before a Bench of Judges and they are divided in opinion, the appeal, with their opinions, shall be laid before another Judge of that Court, and that Judge, after such hearing as he thinks fit, shall deliver his opinion, and the judgment or order shall follow that opinion: Provided that if one of the Judges constituting the Bench, or, where the appeal is laid before another Judge under this section, that Judge, so requires, the appeal shall be re-heard and decided by a larger Bench of Judges.
summary: When a case is being heard by a group of judges in a higher court, and they cannot agree on a decision, the case is sent to another judge for their opinion. This new judge will listen to the case again and make a decision based on their opinion. However, if any of the original judges or the new judge wants a more thorough review, the case can be sent to a larger group of judges for a final decision.

subsections.text: When an appeal under this Chapter is heard by a High Court before a Bench of Judges and they are divided in opinion, the appeal, with their opinions, shall be laid before another Judge of that Court, and that Judge, after such hearing as he thinks fit, shall deliver his opinion, and the judgment or order shall follow that opinion: Provided that if one of the Judges constituting the Bench, or, where the appeal is laid before another Judge under this section, that Judge, so requires, the appeal shall be re-heard and decided by a larger Bench of Judges.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXI: APPEALS
title: Finality of judgments and orders on appeal.
content: Judgments and orders passed by an Appellate Court upon an appeal shall be final, except in the cases provided for in section 418, section 419, sub-section (4) of section 425 or Chapter XXXII: Provided that notwithstanding the final disposal of an appeal against conviction in any case, the Appellate Court may hear and dispose of, on the merits, (a) an appeal against acquittal under section 419, arising out of the same case; or (b) an appeal for the enhancement of sentence under section 418, arising out of the same case.
summary:
When a higher court, known as the Appellate Court, makes a decision on an appeal, it is considered final, unless there are certain exceptions. These exceptions include cases where the Appellate Court has the power to review its own decision under specific sections of the law. 

Even if the Appellate Court has already made a decision on a case, it can still hear and make a new decision on two types of appeals: 

- An appeal by the prosecution to challenge the acquittal (when the court found the person not guilty) of the accused, if the case is related to the same incident. 
- An appeal by the prosecution to increase the sentence of the convicted person, if the case is related to the same incident.

subsections.text: Judgments and orders passed by an Appellate Court upon an appeal shall be final, except in the cases provided for in section 418, section 419, sub-section (4) of section 425 or Chapter XXXII: Provided that notwithstanding the final disposal of an appeal against conviction in any case, the Appellate Court may hear and dispose of, on the merits,
an appeal against acquittal under section 419, arising out of the same case;
an appeal for the enhancement of sentence under section 418, arising out of the same case.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXI: APPEALS
title: Abatement of appeals.
content: (1) Every appeal under section 418 or section 419 shall finally abate on the death of the accused. (2) Every other appeal under this Chapter (except an appeal from a sentence of fine) shall finally abate on the death of the appellant: Provided that where the appeal is against a conviction and sentence of death or of imprisonment, and the appellant dies during the pendency of the appeal, any of his near relatives may, within thirty days of the death of the appellant, apply to the Appellate Court for leave to continue the appeal; and if leave is granted, the appeal shall not abate. Explanation.In this section, “near relative” means a parent, spouse, lineal descendant, brother or sister. 149 CHAPTER XXXII REFERENCE AND REVISION
summary:
If the accused person dies during an appeal, the appeal will automatically end. 

If the person who filed the appeal (the appellant) dies, the appeal will also end, unless:

- The appeal is against a death sentence or a long prison sentence.
- The appellant's close family members (parents, spouse, children, brothers, or sisters) apply to the court within 30 days of the appellant's death to continue the appeal.
- The court grants permission for the appeal to continue.

subsections.text: Every appeal under section 418 or section 419 shall finally abate on the death of the accused.

Every other appeal under this Chapter (except an appeal from a sentence of fine) shall finally abate on the death of the appellant: Provided that where the appeal is against a conviction and sentence of death or of imprisonment, and the appellant dies during the pendency of the appeal, any of his near relatives may, within thirty days of the death of the appellant, apply to the Appellate Court for leave to continue the appeal; and if leave is granted, the appeal shall not abate.
In this section, “near relative” means a parent, spouse, lineal descendant, brother or sister.
CHAPTER XXXII REFERENCE AND REVISION

chapter: CHAPTER XXXII: REFERENCE AND REVISION
title: Reference to High Court.
content: (1) Where any Court is satisfied that a case pending before it involves a question as to the validity of any Act, Ordinance or Regulation or of any provision contained in an Act, Ordinance or Regulation, the determination of which is necessary for the disposal of the case, and is of opinion that such Act, Ordinance, Regulation or provision is invalid or inoperative, but has not been so declared by the High Court to which that Court is subordinate or by the Supreme Court, the Court shall state a case setting out its opinion and the reasons therefor, and refer the same for the decision of the High Court. Explanation.In this section, “Regulation” means any Regulation as defined in the General Act, 1897 (10 of 1897), or in the General Act of a State. (2) A Court of Session may, if it thinks fit in any case pending before it to which the provisions of subsection (1) do not apply, refer for the decision of the High Court any question of law arising in the hearing of such case. (3) Any Court making a reference to the High Court under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2) may, pending the decision of the High Court thereon, either commit the accused to jail or release him on bail to appear when called upon.
summary:
If a court is dealing with a case that involves a question about the validity of a law or regulation, and it thinks the law or regulation is invalid, but hasn't been declared so by a higher court, it can send the case to the High Court for a decision. 

A Court of Session can also send a question of law to the High Court for a decision, even if the case doesn't involve a law or regulation. 

Before sending the case to the High Court, the court can decide whether to keep the accused in jail or release them on bail, depending on the situation.

subsections.text: Where any Court is satisfied that a case pending before it involves a question as to the validity of any Act, Ordinance or Regulation or of any provision contained in an Act, Ordinance or Regulation, the determination of which is necessary for the disposal of the case, and is of opinion that such Act, Ordinance, Regulation or provision is invalid or inoperative, but has not been so declared by the High Court to which that Court is subordinate or by the Supreme Court, the Court shall state a case setting out its opinion and the reasons therefor, and refer the same for the decision of the High Court.
In this section, “Regulation” means any Regulation as defined in the General Act, 1897 (10 of 1897), or in the General Act of a State.

A Court of Session may, if it thinks fit in any case pending before it to which the provisions of subsection (1) do not apply, refer for the decision of the High Court any question of law arising in the hearing of such case.

Any Court making a reference to the High Court under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2) may, pending the decision of the High Court thereon, either commit the accused to jail or release him on bail to appear when called upon.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXII: REFERENCE AND REVISION
title: Disposal of case according to decision of High Court.
content: (1) When a question has been so referred, the High Court shall pass such order thereon as it thinks fit, and shall cause a copy of such order to be sent to the Court by which the reference was made, which shall dispose of the case conformably to the said order. (2) The High Court may direct by whom the costs of such reference shall be paid.
summary: When a High Court makes a decision on a case that was referred to it, it will pass an order based on that decision. The order will then be sent to the lower court that originally referred the case. The lower court must follow the High Court's order and dispose of the case accordingly. The High Court may also decide who should pay the costs of the reference process.

subsections.text: When a question has been so referred, the High Court shall pass such order thereon as it thinks fit, and shall cause a copy of such order to be sent to the Court by which the reference was made, which shall dispose of the case conformably to the said order.
The High Court may direct by whom the costs of such reference shall be paid.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXII: REFERENCE AND REVISION
title: Calling for records to exercise powers of revision.
content: (1) The High Court or any Sessions Judge may call for and examine the record of any proceeding before any inferior Criminal Court situate within its or his local jurisdiction for the purpose of satisfying itself or himself as to the correctness, legality or propriety of any finding, sentence or order, recorded or passed, and as to the regularity of any proceedings of such inferior Court, and may, when calling, for such record, direct that the execution of any sentence or order be suspended, and if the accused is in confinement that he be released on his own bond or bail bond pending the examination of the record. Explanation.All Magistrates, whether Executive or Judicial, and whether exercising original or appellate jurisdiction, shall be deemed to be inferior to the Sessions Judge for the purposes of this subsection and of section 439. (2) The powers of revision conferred by sub-section (1) shall not be exercised in relation to any interlocutory order passed in any appeal, inquiry, trial or other proceeding. (3) If an application under this section has been made by any person either to the High Court or to the Sessions Judge, no further application by the same person shall be entertained by the other of them.
summary:
The High Court or a Sessions Judge can review a case decided by a lower court to ensure the decision was correct, fair, and followed the law. They can ask for the case records and even stop the execution of a sentence or order if necessary. This review can be done to check if the lower court made a mistake or acted unfairly. 

Magistrates, who are lower-level judges, are also considered lower courts for this purpose. However, the High Court or Sessions Judge cannot review every decision made by a lower court, only those that are final. If someone has already applied for a review to one of these courts, they cannot apply again to the other one.

subsections.text: The High Court or any Sessions Judge may call for and examine the record of any proceeding before any inferior Criminal Court situate within its or his local jurisdiction for the purpose of satisfying itself or himself as to the correctness, legality or propriety of any finding, sentence or order, recorded or passed, and as to the regularity of any proceedings of such inferior Court, and may, when calling, for such record, direct that the execution of any sentence or order be suspended, and if the accused is in confinement that he be released on his own bond or bail bond pending the examination of the record.

All Magistrates, whether Executive or Judicial, and whether exercising original or appellate jurisdiction, shall be deemed to be inferior to the Sessions Judge for the purposes of this subsection and of section 439.
The powers of revision conferred by sub-section (1) shall not be exercised in relation to any interlocutory order passed in any appeal, inquiry, trial or other proceeding.

If an application under this section has been made by any person either to the High Court or to the Sessions Judge, no further application by the same person shall be entertained by the other of them.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXII: REFERENCE AND REVISION
title: Power to order inquiry.
content: On examining any record under section 438 or otherwise, the High Court or the Sessions Judge may direct the Chief Judicial Magistrate by himself or by any of the Magistrates subordinate to him to make, and the Chief Judicial Magistrate may himself make or direct any subordinate Magistrate to make, further inquiry into any complaint which has been dismissed under section 226 or subsection (4) of section 227, or into the case of any person accused of an offence who has been discharged: Provided that no Court shall make any direction under this section for inquiry into the case of any person who has been discharged unless such person has had an opportunity of showing cause why such direction should not be made. 150
summary:
If a High Court or Sessions Judge reviews a case record and finds that a complaint was dismissed unfairly or that someone was wrongly discharged from a case, they can order a new investigation. 

This new investigation will be led by the Chief Judicial Magistrate or a lower-ranking Magistrate. However, before making this order, the person who was discharged must be given a chance to explain why they shouldn't be investigated further.

subsections.text: On examining any record under section 438 or otherwise, the High Court or the Sessions Judge may direct the Chief Judicial Magistrate by himself or by any of the Magistrates subordinate to him to make, and the Chief Judicial Magistrate may himself make or direct any subordinate Magistrate to make, further inquiry into any complaint which has been dismissed under section 226 or subsection (4) of section 227, or into the case of any person accused of an offence who has been discharged: Provided that no Court shall make any direction under this section for inquiry into the case of any person who has been discharged unless such person has had an opportunity of showing cause why such direction should not be made.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXII: REFERENCE AND REVISION
title: Sessions Judge's powers of revision.
content: (1) In the case of any proceeding the record of which has been called for by himself, the Sessions Judge may exercise all or any of the powers which may be exercised by the High Court under sub-section (1) of section 442. (2) Where any proceeding by way of revision is commenced before a Sessions Judge under sub-section (1), the provisions of sub-sections (2), (3), (4) and (5) of section 442 shall, so far as may be, apply to such proceeding and references in the said sub-sections to the High Court shall be construed as references to the Sessions Judge. (3) Where any application for revision is made by or on behalf of any person before the Sessions Judge, the decision of the Sessions Judge thereon in relation to such person shall be final and no further proceeding by way of revision at the instance of such person shall be entertained by the High Court or any other Court.
summary:
A Sessions Judge in India has the power to review and correct decisions made by lower courts in certain cases. 

If a Sessions Judge asks for the court records, they can take over some of the High Court's powers to review and correct the decision. 

When a Sessions Judge reviews a case, the rules for the High Court's review process apply, but with the Sessions Judge as the decision-maker instead. 

If someone applies to the Sessions Judge to review a decision, the Sessions Judge's decision on this application is final and cannot be appealed to the High Court or any other court.

subsections.text: In the case of any proceeding the record of which has been called for by himself, the Sessions Judge may exercise all or any of the powers which may be exercised by the High Court under sub-section (1) of section 442.

Where any proceeding by way of revision is commenced before a Sessions Judge under sub-section (1), the provisions of sub-sections (2), (3), (4) and (5) of section 442 shall, so far as may be, apply to such proceeding and references in the said sub-sections to the High Court shall be construed as references to the Sessions Judge.

Where any application for revision is made by or on behalf of any person before the Sessions Judge, the decision of the Sessions Judge thereon in relation to such person shall be final and no further proceeding by way of revision at the instance of such person shall be entertained by the High Court or any other Court.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXII: REFERENCE AND REVISION
title: Power of Additional Sessions Judge.
content: An Additional Sessions Judge shall have and may exercise all the powers of a Sessions Judge under this Chapter in respect of any case which may be transferred to him by or under any general or special order of the Sessions Judge.
summary: An Additional Sessions Judge has the same powers as a Sessions Judge in cases transferred to them. This means they can handle and make decisions in cases that were originally assigned to another Sessions Judge, but have been moved to them due to a court order.

subsections.text: An Additional Sessions Judge shall have and may exercise all the powers of a Sessions Judge under this Chapter in respect of any case which may be transferred to him by or under any general or special order of the Sessions Judge.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXII: REFERENCE AND REVISION
title: High Court's powers of revision.
content: (1) In the case of any proceeding the record of which has been called for by itself or which otherwise comes to its knowledge, the High Court may, in its discretion, exercise any of the powers conferred on a Court of Appeal by sections 427, 430, 431 and 432 or on a Court of Session by section 344, and, when the Judges composing the Court of revision are equally divided in opinion, the case shall be disposed of in the manner provided by section 433. (2) No order under this section shall be made to the prejudice of the accused or other person unless he has had an opportunity of being heard either personally or by advocate in his own defence. (3) Nothing in this section shall be deemed to authorise a High Court to convert a finding of acquittal into one of conviction. (4) Where under this Sanhita an appeal lies and no appeal is brought, no proceeding by way of revision shall be entertained at the instance of the party who could have appealed. (5) Where under this Sanhita an appeal lies but an application for revision has been made to the High Court by any person and the High Court is satisfied that such application was made under the erroneous belief that no appeal lies thereto and that it is necessary in the interests of justice so to do, the High Court may treat the application for revision as a petition of appeal and deal with the same accordingly.
summary:
The High Court in India has the power to review and correct decisions made by lower courts. 

If the High Court finds out about a case or if a case is sent to it for review, it can:

- Review the case and make new decisions, similar to an appeal court.
- If the judges are equally divided on a decision, the case will be decided according to a specific rule.
- Before making any changes that might harm someone, the High Court must give them a chance to be heard or represented by a lawyer.
- The High Court cannot change a decision of 'not guilty' to 'guilty'.
- If someone could have appealed a decision but didn't, the High Court won't consider a revision of that decision.
- If someone applied for a revision thinking they couldn't appeal, the High Court can treat the revision as an appeal and deal with it accordingly.

subsections.text: In the case of any proceeding the record of which has been called for by itself or which otherwise comes to its knowledge, the High Court may, in its discretion, exercise any of the powers conferred on a Court of Appeal by sections 427, 430, 431 and 432 or on a Court of Session by section 344, and, when the Judges composing the Court of revision are equally divided in opinion, the case shall be disposed of in the manner provided by section 433.
No order under this section shall be made to the prejudice of the accused or other person unless he has had an opportunity of being heard either personally or by advocate in his own defence.
Nothing in this section shall be deemed to authorise a High Court to convert a finding of acquittal into one of conviction.
Where under this Sanhita an appeal lies and no appeal is brought, no proceeding by way of revision shall be entertained at the instance of the party who could have appealed.

Where under this Sanhita an appeal lies but an application for revision has been made to the High Court by any person and the High Court is satisfied that such application was made under the erroneous belief that no appeal lies thereto and that it is necessary in the interests of justice so to do,
the High Court may treat the application for revision as a petition of appeal and deal with the same accordingly.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXII: REFERENCE AND REVISION
title: Power of High Court to withdraw or transfer revision cases.
content: (1) Whenever one or more persons convicted at the same trial makes or make application to a High Court for revision and any other person convicted at the same trial makes an application to the Sessions Judge for revision, the High Court shall decide, having regard to the general convenience of the parties and the importance of the questions involved, which of the two Courts should finally dispose of the applications for revision and when the High Court decides that all the applications for revision should be disposed of by itself, the High Court shall direct that the applications for revision pending before the Sessions Judge be transferred to itself and where the High Court decides that it is not necessary for it to dispose of the applications for revision, it shall direct that the applications for revision made to it be transferred to the Sessions Judge. (2) Whenever any application for revision is transferred to the High Court, that Court shall deal with the same as if it were an application duly made before itself. (3) Whenever any application for revision is transferred to the Sessions Judge, that Judge shall deal with the same as if it were an application duly made before himself. (4) Where an application for revision is transferred by the High Court to the Sessions Judge, no further application for revision shall lie to the High Court or to any other Court at the instance of the person or persons whose applications for revision have been disposed of by the Sessions Judge. 151
summary:
If multiple people are convicted in the same trial and some of them apply to the High Court for revision, while others apply to the Sessions Judge, the High Court will decide which court should handle the case. This decision is based on the convenience of the parties involved and the complexity of the issues.

If the High Court decides to handle the case, it will transfer the applications from the Sessions Judge to itself. The High Court will then deal with the case as if the applications were made directly to it.

If the High Court decides not to handle the case, it will transfer the applications made to it to the Sessions Judge. The Sessions Judge will then deal with the case as if the applications were made directly to him.

Once the Sessions Judge has disposed of an application for revision, no further application for revision can be made to the High Court or any other court on behalf of the person or people whose applications were disposed of by the Sessions Judge.

subsections.text: Whenever one or more persons convicted at the same trial makes or make application to a High Court for revision and any other person convicted at the same trial makes an application to the Sessions Judge for revision, the High Court shall decide, having regard to the general convenience of the parties and the importance of the questions involved, which of the two Courts should finally dispose of the applications for revision and when the High Court decides that all the applications for revision should be disposed of by itself, the High Court shall direct that the applications for revision pending before the Sessions Judge be transferred to itself and where the High Court decides that it is not necessary for it to dispose of the applications for revision, it shall direct that the applications for revision made to it be transferred to the Sessions Judge.
Whenever any application for revision is transferred to the High Court, that Court shall deal with the same as if it were an application duly made before itself.
Whenever any application for revision is transferred to the Sessions Judge, that Judge shall deal with the same as if it were an application duly made before himself.

Where an application for revision is transferred by the High Court to the Sessions Judge, no further application for revision shall lie to the High Court or to any other Court at the instance of the person or persons whose applications for revision have been disposed of by the Sessions Judge.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXII: REFERENCE AND REVISION
title: Option of Court to hear parties.
content: Save as otherwise expressly provided by this Sanhita, no party has any right to be heard either personally or by an advocate before any Court exercising its powers of revision; but the Court may, if it thinks fit, when exercising such powers, hear any party either personally or by an advocate.
summary: In general, when a court reviews a case, it usually doesn't allow the parties involved to be present or have a lawyer represent them. However, the court has the discretion to decide if it wants to hear from the parties or their lawyers during this review process. This means that even if the law doesn't require the court to listen to the parties, it can still choose to do so if it thinks it's necessary.

subsections.text: Save as otherwise expressly provided by this Sanhita, no party has any right to be heard either personally or by an advocate before any Court exercising its powers of revision; but the Court may, if it thinks fit, when exercising such powers, hear any party either personally or by an advocate.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXII: REFERENCE AND REVISION
title: High Court's order to be certified to lower Court.
content: When a case is revised under this Chapter by the High Court or a Sessions Judge, it or he shall, in the manner provided by section 429, certify its decision or order to the Court by which the finding, sentence or order revised was recorded or passed, and the Court to which the decision or order is so certified shall thereupon make such orders as are conformable to the decision so certified, and, if necessary, the record shall be amended in accordance therewith. CHAPTER XXXIII TRANSFER OF CRIMINAL CASES
summary: When a higher court like the High Court or a Sessions Judge reviews and changes a decision made by a lower court, they must officially confirm this new decision in writing. This written confirmation is then sent to the lower court that made the original decision. Once the lower court receives this confirmation, they must follow the new decision and make any necessary changes to the case records to reflect the updated outcome.

subsections.text: When a case is revised under this Chapter by the High Court or a Sessions Judge, it or he shall, in the manner provided by section 429, certify its decision or order to the Court by which the finding, sentence or order revised was recorded or passed, and the Court to which the decision or order is so certified shall thereupon make such orders as are conformable to the decision so certified, and, if necessary, the record shall be amended in accordance therewith.
TRANSFER OF CRIMINAL CASES

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIII: TRANSFER OF CRIMINAL CASES
title: Power of Supreme Court to transfer cases and appeals.
content: (1) Whenever it is made to appear to the Supreme Court that an order under this section is expedient for the ends of justice, it may direct that any particular case or appeal be transferred from one High Court to another High Court or from a Criminal Court subordinate to one High Court to another Criminal Court of equal or superior jurisdiction subordinate to another High Court. (2) The Supreme Court may act under this section only on the application of the Attorney-General of India or of a party interested, and every such application shall be made by motion, which shall, except when the applicant is the Attorney-General of India or the Advocate-General of the State, be supported by affidavit or affirmation. (3) Where any application for the exercise of the powers conferred by this section is dismissed, the Supreme Court may, if it is of opinion that the application was frivolous or vexatious, order the applicant to pay by way of compensation to any person who has opposed the application such sum as it may consider appropriate in the circumstances of the case.
summary:
The Supreme Court of India has the power to transfer cases or appeals from one High Court to another or from one lower court to another lower court if it thinks it's necessary for justice to be served. 

To do this, the Attorney-General of India or a person directly involved in the case must ask the Supreme Court in a formal motion. If the person asking is not the Attorney-General or the State's top lawyer, they must also provide a sworn statement to support their request.

If the Supreme Court rejects the request, they can order the person who made the request to pay compensation to anyone who opposed the request, if they think the request was made without a good reason or to cause trouble.

subsections.text: Whenever it is made to appear to the Supreme Court that an order under this section is expedient for the ends of justice, it may direct that any particular case or appeal be transferred from one High Court to another High Court or from a Criminal Court subordinate to one High Court to another Criminal Court of equal or superior jurisdiction subordinate to another High Court.

The Supreme Court may act under this section only on the application of the Attorney-General of India or of a party interested, and every such application shall be made by motion, which shall, except when the applicant is the Attorney-General of India or the Advocate-General of the State, be supported by affidavit or affirmation.

Where any application for the exercise of the powers conferred by this section is dismissed, the Supreme Court may, if it is of opinion that the application was frivolous or vexatious, order the applicant to pay by way of compensation to any person who has opposed the application such sum as it may consider appropriate in the circumstances of the case.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIII: TRANSFER OF CRIMINAL CASES
title: Power of High Court to transfer cases and appeals.
content: (1) Whenever it is made to appear to the High Court (a) that a fair and impartial inquiry or trial cannot be had in any Criminal Court subordinate thereto; or (b) that some question of law of unusual difficulty is likely to arise; or (c) that an order under this section is required by any provision of this Sanhita, or will tend to the general convenience of the parties or witnesses, or is expedient for the ends of justice, it may order (i) that any offence be inquired into or tried by any Court not qualified under sections 197 to 205 (both inclusive), but in other respects competent to inquire into or try such offence; (ii) that any particular case or appeal, or class of cases or appeals, be transferred from a Criminal Court subordinate to its authority to any other such Criminal Court of equal or superior jurisdiction; (iii) that any particular case be committed for trial to a Court of Session; or (iv) that any particular case or appeal be transferred to and tried before itself. (2) The High Court may act either on the report of the lower Court, or on the application of a party interested, or on its own initiative: Provided that no application shall lie to the High Court for transferring a case from one Criminal Court to another Criminal Court in the same sessions division, unless an application for such transfer has been made to the Sessions Judge and rejected by him. 152 (3) Every application for an order under sub-section (1) shall be made by motion, which shall, except when the applicant is the Advocate-General of the State, be supported by affidavit or affirmation. (4) When such application is made by an accused person, the High Court may direct him to execute a bond or bail bond for the payment of any compensation which the High Court may award under sub-section (7). (5) Every accused person making such application shall give to the Public Prosecutor notice in writing of the application, together with a copy of the grounds on which it is made; and no order shall be made on the merits of the application unless at least twenty-four hours have elapsed between the giving of such notice and the hearing of the application. (6) Where the application is for the transfer of a case or appeal from any subordinate Court, the High Court may, if it is satisfied that it is necessary so to do in the interest of justice, order that, pending the disposal of the application the proceedings in the subordinate Court shall be stayed, on such terms as the High Court may think fit to impose: Provided that such stay shall not affect the subordinate Court's power of remand under section 346. (7) Where an application for an order under sub-section (1) is dismissed, the High Court may, if it is of opinion that the application was frivolous or vexatious, order the applicant to pay by way of compensation to any person who has opposed the application such sum as it may consider proper in the circumstances of the case. (8) When the High Court orders under sub-section (1) that a case be transferred from any Court for trial before itself, it shall observe in such trial the same procedure which that Court would have observed if the case had not been so transferred. (9) Nothing in this section shall be deemed to affect any order of the Government under section 218.
summary:
The High Court has the power to transfer cases and appeals from one court to another if it feels that a fair trial cannot be held in the original court, or if a complex legal issue is involved, or if it's necessary for the convenience of the parties or witnesses, or for the sake of justice. The High Court can order a case to be tried by a different court, or transfer a case to itself for trial. 

The High Court can take this decision on its own, or based on a report from a lower court, or on an application from a party involved in the case. However, if a party wants to transfer a case from one court to another within the same sessions division, they must first apply to the Sessions Judge and get rejected before approaching the High Court.

To apply for a transfer, a party must make a motion to the High Court, usually supported by an affidavit or affirmation. If the application is made by the accused person, the High Court may ask them to provide a bond or bail bond to cover any potential compensation that may be awarded.

The accused person must also give notice to the Public Prosecutor and provide a copy of the grounds for the application. The High Court may stay the proceedings in the lower court until the application is decided, but this will not affect the lower court's power to remand the accused.

If the application is dismissed and the High Court feels that it was frivolous or vexatious, it may order the applicant to pay compensation to the opposing party. If the High Court decides to try a case itself after transferring it, it will follow the same procedure that the original court would have followed.

subsections.text: Whenever it is made to appear to the High Court

Whenever it is made to appear to the High Court (a) that a fair and impartial inquiry or trial cannot be had in any Criminal Court subordinate thereto; or (b) that some question of law of unusual difficulty is likely to arise; or (c) that an order under this section is required by any provision of this Sanhita, or will tend to the general convenience of the parties or witnesses, or is expedient for the ends of justice, it may order (i) that any offence be inquired into or tried by any Court not qualified under sections 197 to 205 (both inclusive), but in other respects competent to inquire into or try such offence; (ii) that any particular case or appeal, or class of cases or appeals, be transferred from a Criminal Court subordinate to its authority to any other such Criminal Court of equal or superior jurisdiction; (iii) that any particular case be committed for trial to a Court of Session; or (iv) that any particular case or appeal be transferred to and tried before itself.
that a fair and impartial inquiry or trial cannot be had in any Criminal Court subordinate thereto;
that some question of law of unusual difficulty is likely to arise;
that an order under this section is required by any provision of this Sanhita, or will tend to the general convenience of the parties or witnesses, or is expedient for the ends of justice,
that any offence be inquired into or tried by any Court not qualified under sections 197 to 205 (both inclusive), but in other respects competent to inquire into or try such offence;
that any particular case or appeal, or class of cases or appeals, be transferred from a Criminal Court subordinate to its authority to any other such Criminal Court of equal or superior jurisdiction;
that any particular case be committed for trial to a Court of Session;
that any particular case or appeal be transferred to and tried before itself.

The High Court may act either on the report of the lower Court, or on the application of a party interested, or on its own initiative: Provided that no application shall lie to the High Court for transferring a case from one Criminal Court to another Criminal Court in the same sessions division, unless an application for such transfer has been made to the Sessions Judge and rejected by him.
Every application for an order under sub-section (1) shall be made by motion, which shall, except when the applicant is the Advocate-General of the State, be supported by affidavit or affirmation.

When such application is made by an accused person, the High Court may direct him to execute a bond or bail bond for the payment of any compensation which the High Court may award under sub-section (7).

Every accused person making such application shall give to the Public Prosecutor notice in writing of the application, together with a copy of the grounds on which it is made; and no order shall be made on the merits of the application unless at least twenty-four hours have elapsed between the giving of such notice and the hearing of the application.

Where the application is for the transfer of a case or appeal from any subordinate Court, the High Court may, if it is satisfied that it is necessary so to do in the interest of justice, order that, pending the disposal of the application the proceedings in the subordinate Court shall be stayed, on such terms as the High Court may think fit to impose: Provided that such stay shall not affect the subordinate Court's power of remand under section 346.

Where an application for an order under sub-section (1) is dismissed, the High Court may, if it is of opinion that the application was frivolous or vexatious, order the applicant to pay by way of compensation to any person who has opposed the application such sum as it may consider proper in the circumstances of the case.

When the High Court orders under sub-section (1) that a case be transferred from any Court for trial before itself, it shall observe in such trial the same procedure which that Court would have observed if the case had not been so transferred.
Nothing in this section shall be deemed to affect any order of the Government under section 218.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIII: TRANSFER OF CRIMINAL CASES
title: Power of Sessions Judge to transfer cases and appeals.
content: (1) Whenever it is made to appear to a Sessions Judge that an order under this sub-section is expedient for the ends of justice, he may order that any particular case be transferred from one Criminal Court to another Criminal Court in his sessions division. (2) The Sessions Judge may act either on the report of the lower Court, or on the application of a party interested, or on his own initiative. (3) The provisions of sub-sections (3), (4), (5), (6), (7) and (9) of section 447 shall apply in relation to an application to the Sessions Judge for an order under sub-section (1) as they apply in relation to an application to the High Court for an order under sub-section (1) of section 447, except that sub-section (7) of that section shall so apply as if for the word “sum” occurring therein, the words “sum not exceeding ten thousand rupees” were substituted.
summary:
A Sessions Judge in India has the power to transfer a case from one court to another within their sessions division if it's necessary for justice to be served. 

This can happen in three ways: 
- If a lower court recommends it.
- If a party involved in the case asks for it.
- If the Sessions Judge decides it's necessary on their own.

When a party wants the case to be transferred, they need to follow certain rules and procedures, similar to those used when asking the High Court to transfer a case. However, the amount of money that can be claimed as a cost for the transfer is limited to 10,000 rupees.

subsections.text: Whenever it is made to appear to a Sessions Judge that an order under this sub-section is expedient for the ends of justice, he may order that any particular case be transferred from one Criminal Court to another Criminal Court in his sessions division.
The Sessions Judge may act either on the report of the lower Court, or on the application of a party interested, or on his own initiative.

The provisions of sub-sections (3), (4), (5), (6), (7) and (9) of section 447 shall apply in relation to an application to the Sessions Judge for an order under sub-section (1) as they apply in relation to an application to the High Court for an order under sub-section (1) of section 447, except that sub-section (7) of that section shall so apply as if for the word “sum” occurring therein, the words “sum not exceeding ten thousand rupees” were substituted.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIII: TRANSFER OF CRIMINAL CASES
title: Withdrawal of cases and appeals by Sessions Judges.
content: (1) A Sessions Judge may withdraw any case or appeal from, or recall any case or appeal which he has made over to a Chief Judicial Magistrate subordinate to him. (2) At any time before the trial of the case or the hearing of the appeal has commenced before the Additional Sessions Judge, a Sessions Judge may recall any case or appeal which he has made over to any Additional Sessions Judge. (3) Where a Sessions Judge withdraws or recalls case or appeal under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2), he may either try the case in his own Court or hear the appeal himself, or make it over in accordance with the provisions of this Sanhita to another Court for trial or hearing, as the case may be. 153
summary:
A Sessions Judge has the power to take back a case or appeal that has been sent to a lower court, such as a Chief Judicial Magistrate or an Additional Sessions Judge. 

This can happen at any stage before the case goes to trial or the appeal is heard. If a Sessions Judge takes back a case, they have three options: 

1. They can try the case themselves in their own court.
2. They can hear the appeal themselves.
3. They can send the case to another court for trial or hearing, following the rules of the BNSS.

subsections.text: A Sessions Judge may withdraw any case or appeal from, or recall any case or appeal which he has made over to a Chief Judicial Magistrate subordinate to him.

At any time before the trial of the case or the hearing of the appeal has commenced before the Additional Sessions Judge, a Sessions Judge may recall any case or appeal which he has made over to any Additional Sessions Judge.

Where a Sessions Judge withdraws or recalls case or appeal under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2), he may either try the case in his own Court or hear the appeal himself, or make it over in accordance with the provisions of this Sanhita to another Court for trial or hearing, as the case may be.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIII: TRANSFER OF CRIMINAL CASES
title: Withdrawal of cases by Judicial Magistrates.
content: (1) Any Chief Judicial Magistrate may withdraw any case from, or recall any case which he has made over to, any Magistrate subordinate to him, and may inquire into or try such case himself, or refer it for inquiry or trial to any other such Magistrate competent to inquire into or try the same. (2) Any Judicial Magistrate may recall any case made over by him under sub-section (2) of section 212 to any other Magistrate and may inquire into or try such cases himself.
summary: In India, if a case is being heard by a lower-level Magistrate, the Chief Judicial Magistrate can take it back and decide it themselves, or pass it on to another lower-level Magistrate who is qualified to handle it. This means the Chief Judicial Magistrate has the power to oversee and manage cases being heard by their subordinates. Additionally, if a Judicial Magistrate has passed a case to another Magistrate, they can also take it back and decide it themselves.

subsections.text: Any Chief Judicial Magistrate may withdraw any case from, or recall any case which he has made over to, any Magistrate subordinate to him, and may inquire into or try such case himself, or refer it for inquiry or trial to any other such Magistrate competent to inquire into or try the same.
Any Judicial Magistrate may recall any case made over by him under sub-section (2) of section 212 to any other Magistrate and may inquire into or try such cases himself.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIII: TRANSFER OF CRIMINAL CASES
title: Making over or withdrawal of cases by Executive Magistrates.
content: Any District Magistrate or Sub-divisional Magistrate may (a) make over, for disposal, any proceeding which has been started before him, to any Magistrate subordinate to him; (b) withdraw any case from, or recall any case which he has made over to, any Magistrate subordinate to him, and dispose of such proceeding himself or refer it for disposal to any other Magistrate.
summary:
In India, Executive Magistrates like District Magistrates or Sub-divisional Magistrates have the power to transfer or withdraw cases from one Magistrate to another. 

If a case is already being heard by a lower-ranking Magistrate, the higher-ranking Magistrate can decide to transfer the case to them for disposal. 

If the higher-ranking Magistrate has already transferred a case to a lower-ranking Magistrate, they can take it back and decide it themselves or send it to another Magistrate for disposal.

subsections.text: Any District Magistrate or Sub-divisional Magistrate may
make over, for disposal, any proceeding which has been started before him,
withdraw any case from, or recall any case which he has made over to, any Magistrate subordinate to him, and dispose of such proceeding himself or refer it for disposal to any other Magistrate.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIII: TRANSFER OF CRIMINAL CASES
title: Reasons to be recorded.
content: A Sessions Judge or Magistrate making an order under section 448, section 449, section 450 or section 451 shall record his reasons for making it. CHAPTER XXXIV EXECUTION, SUSPENSION, REMISSION AND COMMUTATION OF SENTENCES A.Death sentences
summary: When a Sessions Judge or Magistrate makes a decision to sentence someone to death, they must write down the reasons behind their decision. This is to ensure transparency and accountability in the judicial process.

subsections.text: A Sessions Judge or Magistrate making an order under section 448, section 449, section 450 or section 451 shall record his reasons for making it.

subsections.id: CHAPTER XXXIV EXECUTION, SUSPENSION, REMISSION AND COMMUTATION OF SENTENCES
subsections.text: A.Death sentences

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIV: EXECUTION, SUSPENSION, REMISSION AND COMMUTATION OF SENTENCES
title: Execution of order passed under section 409.
content: When in a case submitted to the High Court for the confirmation of a sentence of death, the Court of Session receives the order of confirmation or other order of the High Court thereon, it shall cause such order to be carried into effect by issuing a warrant or taking such other steps as may be necessary.
summary: When the High Court confirms a death sentence, the Court of Session will receive the order. They will then make sure the order is carried out by issuing a warrant or taking any other necessary steps. This means that once the High Court has made the final decision, the lower court will take action to execute the sentence.

subsections.text: When in a case submitted to the High Court for the confirmation of a sentence of death, the Court of Session receives the order of confirmation or other order of the High Court thereon, it shall cause such order to be carried into effect by issuing a warrant or taking such other steps as may be necessary.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIV: EXECUTION, SUSPENSION, REMISSION AND COMMUTATION OF SENTENCES
title: Execution of sentence of death passed by High Court.
content: When a sentence of death is passed by the High Court in appeal or in revision, the Court of Session shall, on receiving the order of the High Court, cause the sentence to be carried into effect by issuing a warrant. 455. Postponement of execution of sentence of death in case of appeal to Supreme Court.(1) Where a person is sentenced to death by the High Court and an appeal from its judgment lies to the Supreme Court under sub-clause (a) or sub-clause (b) of clause (1) of article 134 of the Constitution, the High Court shall order the execution of the sentence to be postponed until the period allowed for preferring such appeal has expired, or if, an appeal is preferred within that period, until such appeal is disposed of. (2) Where a sentence of death is passed or confirmed by the High Court, and the person sentenced makes an application to the High Court for the grant of a certificate under article 132 or under sub-clause (c) of clause (1) of article 134 of the Constitution, the High Court shall order the execution of the sentence to be postponed until such application is disposed of by the High Court, or if a certificate is granted on such application, until the period allowed for preferring an appeal to the Supreme Court on such certificate has expired. (3) Where a sentence of death is passed or confirmed by the High Court, and the High Court is satisfied that the person sentenced intends to present a petition to the Supreme Court for the grant of special leave to appeal under article 136 of the Constitution, the High Court shall order the execution of the sentence to be postponed for such period as it considers sufficient to enable him to present such petition.
summary:
When a High Court passes a death sentence, the Court of Session will carry out the sentence by issuing a warrant. However, if the person sentenced appeals to the Supreme Court, the execution of the sentence will be postponed until the appeal is disposed of or the time allowed for appealing has expired. 

There are three situations where the execution of the sentence will be postponed:

1. If the person sentenced appeals to the Supreme Court within the allowed time, the execution will be postponed until the appeal is disposed of.
2. If the person sentenced applies to the High Court for a certificate to appeal to the Supreme Court, the execution will be postponed until the application is disposed of or a certificate is granted and the time allowed for appealing has expired.
3. If the person sentenced intends to file a petition to the Supreme Court for special leave to appeal, the High Court can postpone the execution of the sentence for a sufficient period to allow the person to file the petition.

subsections.text: When a sentence of death is passed by the High Court in appeal or in revision, the Court of Session shall, on receiving the order of the High Court, cause the sentence to be carried into effect by issuing a warrant.

Where a person is sentenced to death by the High Court and an appeal from its judgment lies to the Supreme Court under sub-clause (a) or sub-clause (b) of clause (1) of article 134 of the Constitution, the High Court shall order the execution of the sentence to be postponed until the period allowed for preferring such appeal has expired, or if, an appeal is preferred within that period, until such appeal is disposed of.

Where a sentence of death is passed or confirmed by the High Court, and the person sentenced makes an application to the High Court for the grant of a certificate under article 132 or under sub-clause (c) of clause (1) of article 134 of the Constitution, the High Court shall order the execution of the sentence to be postponed until such application is disposed of by the High Court, or if a certificate is granted on such application, until the period allowed for preferring an appeal to the Supreme Court on such certificate has expired.

Where a sentence of death is passed or confirmed by the High Court, and the High Court is satisfied that the person sentenced intends to present a petition to the Supreme Court for the grant of special leave to appeal under article 136 of the Constitution, the High Court shall order the execution of the sentence to be postponed for such period as it considers sufficient to enable him to present such petition.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIV: EXECUTION, SUSPENSION, REMISSION AND COMMUTATION OF SENTENCES
title: Postponement of execution of sentence of death in case of appeal to Supreme Court.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIV: EXECUTION, SUSPENSION, REMISSION AND COMMUTATION OF SENTENCES
title: Commutation of sentence of death on pregnant woman.
content: If a woman sentenced to death is found to be pregnant, the High Court shall commute the sentence to imprisonment for life. 154 B.Imprisonment
summary: If a woman who has been sentenced to death is found to be pregnant, the High Court will change her sentence to life imprisonment instead of the death penalty. This means she will serve a life sentence in prison instead of being put to death.

subsections.text: If a woman sentenced to death is found to be pregnant, the High Court shall commute the sentence to imprisonment for life.
Imprisonment

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIV: EXECUTION, SUSPENSION, REMISSION AND COMMUTATION OF SENTENCES
title: Power to appoint place of imprisonment.
content: (1) Except when otherwise provided by any law for the time being in force, the State Government may direct in what place any person liable to be imprisoned or committed to custody under this Sanhita shall be confined. (2) If any person liable to be imprisoned or committed to custody under this Sanhita is in confinement in a civil jail, the Court or Magistrate ordering the imprisonment or committal may direct that the person be removed to a criminal jail. (3) When a person is removed to a criminal jail under sub-section (2), he shall, on being released therefrom, be sent back to the civil jail, unless either (a) three years have elapsed since he was removed to the criminal jail, in which case he shall be deemed to have been released from the civil jail under section 58 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908); or (b) the Court which ordered his imprisonment in the civil jail has certified to the officer in charge of the criminal jail that he is entitled to be released under section 58 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908).
summary:
When someone is sentenced to imprisonment under the BNSS, the state government can decide where they will be kept, unless there's a specific law that says otherwise. 

If someone is already in a civil jail and gets sentenced to imprisonment, the court or magistrate can order them to be transferred to a criminal jail. When they are released from the criminal jail, they will be sent back to the civil jail, unless it's been three years since they were transferred, or the court that originally sentenced them certifies that they should be released.

subsections.text: Except when otherwise provided by any law for the time being in force, the State Government may direct in what place any person liable to be imprisoned or committed to custody under this Sanhita shall be confined.

If any person liable to be imprisoned or committed to custody under this Sanhita is in confinement in a civil jail, the Court or Magistrate ordering the imprisonment or committal may direct that the person be removed to a criminal jail.
When a person is removed to a criminal jail under sub-section (2), he shall, on being released therefrom, be sent back to the civil jail, unless either

three years have elapsed since he was removed to the criminal jail, in which case he shall be deemed to have been released from the civil jail under section 58 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908);

the Court which ordered his imprisonment in the civil jail has certified to the officer in charge of the criminal jail that he is entitled to be released under section 58 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908)

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIV: EXECUTION, SUSPENSION, REMISSION AND COMMUTATION OF SENTENCES
title: Execution of sentence of imprisonment.
