Provided further that the High Court or the Court of Session shall, before granting bail to a person who is accused of an offence triable under section 65 or sub-section (2) of section 70 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, give notice of the application for bail to the Public Prosecutor within a period of fifteen days from the date of receipt of the notice of such application.

The presence of the informant or any person authorised by him shall be obligatory at the time of hearing of the application for bail to the person under section 65 or sub-section (2) of section 70 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
A High Court or Court of Session may direct that any person who has been released on bail under this Chapter be arrested and commit him to custody.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Amount of bond and reduction thereof.
content: (1) The amount of every bond executed under this Chapter shall be fixed with due regard to the circumstances of the case and shall not be excessive. (2) The High Court or the Court of Session may direct that the bail required by a police officer or Magistrate be reduced.
summary:
When a person is granted bail, they are required to pay a certain amount of money as a bond. This amount should be fair and not too high, considering the specific circumstances of the case. 

If the police or a Magistrate asks for a certain amount of money as bail, the High Court or the Court of Session can review this amount and decide if it's too high. If they think so, they can reduce the bail amount to make it more reasonable.

subsections.text: The amount of every bond executed under this Chapter shall be fixed with due regard to the circumstances of the case and shall not be excessive.
The High Court or the Court of Session may direct that the bail required by a police officer or Magistrate be reduced.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Bond of accused and sureties.
content: (1) Before any person is released on bond or bail bond, a bond for such sum of money as the police officer or Court, as the case may be, thinks sufficient shall be executed by such person, and, when he is released on bond or bail bond, by one or more sufficient sureties conditioned that such person shall attend at the time and place mentioned in the bond, and shall continue so to attend until otherwise directed by the police officer or Court, as the case may be. (2) Where any condition is imposed for the release of any person on bail, the bond or bail bond shall also contain that condition. (3) If the case so requires, the bond or bail bond shall also bind the person released on bail to appear when called upon at the High Court, Court of Session or other Court to answer the charge. (4) For the purpose of determining whether the sureties are fit or sufficient, the Court may accept affidavits in proof of the facts contained therein relating to the sufficiency or fitness of the sureties, or, if it considers necessary, may either hold an enquiry itself or cause an inquiry to be made by a Magistrate subordinate to the Court, as to such sufficiency or fitness. 163
summary:
When someone is arrested and released on bail, they and one or more sureties (people who promise to guarantee their behavior) must sign a bond. This bond promises that the person will attend court at the required time and place, and will continue to do so until told otherwise by the police or court. 

The bond also includes any conditions set by the court for the person's release on bail. If the court requires it, the bond may also promise that the person will appear in a higher court to answer the charges against them.

To ensure the sureties are trustworthy and able to guarantee the person's behavior, the court may ask for proof, such as affidavits, or hold an inquiry to check their fitness and sufficiency.

subsections.text: Before any person is released on bond or bail bond, a bond for such sum of money as the police officer or Court, as the case may be, thinks sufficient shall be executed by such person, and, when he is released on bond or bail bond, by one or more sufficient sureties conditioned that such person shall attend at the time and place mentioned in the bond, and shall continue so to attend until otherwise directed by the police officer or Court, as the case may be.
Where any condition is imposed for the release of any person on bail, the bond or bail bond shall also contain that condition.
If the case so requires, the bond or bail bond shall also bind the person released on bail to appear when called upon at the High Court, Court of Session or other Court to answer the charge.

For the purpose of determining whether the sureties are fit or sufficient, the Court may accept affidavits in proof of the facts contained therein relating to the sufficiency or fitness of the sureties, or, if it considers necessary, may either hold an enquiry itself or cause an inquiry to be made by a Magistrate subordinate to the Court, as to such sufficiency or fitness.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Declaration by sureties.
content: Every person standing surety to an accused person for his release on bail, shall make a declaration before the Court as to the number of persons to whom he has stood surety including the accused, giving therein all the relevant particulars.
summary: When someone is granted bail, they need one or more people to act as sureties, promising that the accused will attend court hearings and follow the law. The surety must tell the court how many people they have stood surety for, including the accused, and provide all the necessary details.

subsections.text: Every person standing surety to an accused person for his release on bail, shall make a declaration before the Court as to the number of persons to whom he has stood surety including the accused, giving therein all the relevant particulars.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Discharge from custody.
content: (1) As soon as the bond or bail bond has been executed, the person for whose appearance it has been executed shall be released; and, when he is in jail, the court admitting him to bail shall issue an order of release to the officer in charge of the jail, and such officer on receipt of the orders shall release him. (2) Nothing in this section, section 478 or section 480, shall be deemed to require the release of any person liable to be detained for some matter other than that in respect of which the bond or bail bond was executed.
summary: When a person posts bail or a bond, they are released from jail immediately. If they were in jail, the court that allowed the bail will send an order to the jail authorities to let them go. The jail authorities will then release the person. However, if the person is being held for another reason, such as a different crime, they will not be released just because they posted bail for a different case.

subsections.text: As soon as the bond or bail bond has been executed, the person for whose appearance it has been executed shall be released; and, when he is in jail, the court admitting him to bail shall issue an order of release to the officer in charge of the jail, and such officer on receipt of the orders shall release him.

Nothing in this section, section 478 or section 480, shall be deemed to require the release of any person liable to be detained for some matter other than that in respect of which the bond or bail bond was executed.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Power to order sufficient bail when that first taken is insufficient.
content: If, through mistake, fraud or otherwise, insufficient sureties have been accepted, or if they afterwards become insufficient, the Court may issue a warrant of arrest directing that the person released on bail be brought before it and may order him to find sufficient sureties, and, on his failing so to do, may commit him to jail.
summary: If a court mistakenly or due to fraud accepts bail sureties that are not enough to secure the person's release, or if the sureties become insufficient later, the court can order the person to be brought back before it. The court can then ask the person to find new, sufficient sureties. If the person fails to do so, the court can send them to jail.

subsections.text: If, through mistake, fraud or otherwise, insufficient sureties have been accepted, or if they afterwards become insufficient, the Court may issue a warrant of arrest directing that the person released on bail be brought before it and may order him to find sufficient sureties, and, on his failing so to do, may commit him to jail.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Discharge of sureties.
content: (1) All or any sureties for the attendance and appearance of a person released on bail may at any time apply to a Magistrate to discharge the bond, either wholly or so far as relates to the applicants. (2) On such application being made, the Magistrate shall issue his warrant of arrest directing that the person so released be brought before him. (3) On the appearance of such person pursuant to the warrant, or on his voluntary surrender, the Magistrate shall direct the bond to be discharged either wholly or so far as relates to the applicants, and shall call upon such person to find other sufficient sureties, and, if he fails to do so, may commit him to jail.
summary:
When someone is released on bail, they may have one or more sureties (people who guarantee their attendance in court). If the sureties want to stop being responsible for the person's attendance, they can ask a Magistrate (a judge) to cancel the bail bond. 

To do this, the sureties need to go to the Magistrate and ask for the bond to be cancelled. The Magistrate will then issue a warrant for the person's arrest, asking them to come to court. 

Once the person appears in court, either because they were arrested or because they chose to surrender, the Magistrate will cancel the bail bond and ask the person to find new sureties. If the person cannot find new sureties, the Magistrate can send them to jail.

subsections.text: All or any sureties for the attendance and appearance of a person released on bail may at any time apply to a Magistrate to discharge the bond, either wholly or so far as relates to the applicants.
On such application being made, the Magistrate shall issue his warrant of arrest directing that the person so released be brought before him.

On the appearance of such person pursuant to the warrant, or on his voluntary surrender, the Magistrate shall direct the bond to be discharged either wholly or so far as relates to the applicants, and shall call upon such person to find other sufficient sureties, and, if he fails to do so, may commit him to jail.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Deposit instead of recognizance.
content: When any person is required by any Court or officer to execute a bond or bail bond, such Court or officer may, except in the case of a bond for good behaviour, permit him to deposit a sum of money or Government promissory notes to such amount as the Court or officer may fix in lieu of executing such bond.
summary: When a court or government officer asks someone to sign a bond or bail bond, they can choose to pay a fixed amount of money instead. This amount is decided by the court or officer, and it's usually used in place of the bond. This option is not available for bonds related to good behaviour.

subsections.text: When any person is required by any Court or officer to execute a bond or bail bond, such Court or officer may, except in the case of a bond for good behaviour, permit him to deposit a sum of money or Government promissory notes to such amount as the Court or officer may fix in lieu of executing such bond.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Procedure when bond has been forfeited.
content: (1) Where, (a) a bond under this Sanhita is for appearance, or for production of property, before a Court and it is proved to the satisfaction of that Court, or of any Court to which the case has subsequently been transferred, that the bond has been forfeited; or (b) in respect of any other bond under this Sanhita, it is proved to the satisfaction of the Court by which the bond was taken, or of any Court to which the case has subsequently been transferred, or of the Court of any Magistrate of the first class, that the bond has been forfeited, the Court shall record the grounds of such proof, and may call upon any person bound by such bond to pay the penalty thereof or to show cause why it should not be paid. Explanation.A condition in a bond for appearance, or for production of property, before a Court shall be construed as including a condition for appearance, or as the case may be, for production of property, before any Court to which the case may subsequently be transferred. (2) If sufficient cause is not shown and the penalty is not paid, the Court may proceed to recover the same as if such penalty were a fine imposed by it under this Sanhita: Provided that where such penalty is not paid and cannot be recovered in the manner aforesaid, the person so bound as surety shall be liable, by order of the Court ordering the recovery of the penalty, to imprisonment in civil jail for a term which may extend to six months. 164 (3) The Court may, after recording its reasons for doing so, remit any portion of the penalty mentioned and enforce payment in part only. (4) Where a surety to a bond dies before the bond is forfeited, his estate shall be discharged from all liability in respect of the bond. (5) Where any person who has furnished security under section 125 or section 136 or section 401 is convicted of an offence the commission of which constitutes a breach of the conditions of his bond, or of a bond executed in lieu of his bond under section 494, a certified copy of the judgment of the Court by which he was convicted of such offence may be used as evidence in proceedings under this section against his surety or sureties, and, if such certified copy is so used, the Court shall presume that such offence was committed by him unless the contrary is proved.
summary:
When a bond is forfeited, the court will record the reasons why it was forfeited and may ask the person who signed the bond to pay the penalty or explain why they shouldn't have to pay it. 

If the person can't show a good reason and doesn't pay the penalty, the court can take the money from them like a fine. If they still can't pay, the person who signed as a surety (like a guarantor) might go to jail for up to six months.

However, if the person who signed the bond dies before it's forfeited, their family or estate won't be responsible for paying the penalty. 

Additionally, if someone who signed a bond for a specific reason (like to ensure they show up to court) commits a crime that breaks the terms of the bond, the court can use a copy of their conviction as evidence against their guarantor. In this case, the court will assume the person committed the crime unless they can prove otherwise.

subsections.text: Where, (a) a bond under this Sanhita is for appearance, or for production of property, before a Court and it is proved to the satisfaction of that Court, or of any Court to which the case has subsequently been transferred, that the bond has been forfeited; or (b) in respect of any other bond under this Sanhita, it is proved to the satisfaction of the Court by which the bond was taken, or of any Court to which the case has subsequently been transferred, or of the Court of any Magistrate of the first class, that the bond has been forfeited, the Court shall record the grounds of such proof, and may call upon any person bound by such bond to pay the penalty thereof or to show cause why it should not be paid.

a bond under this Sanhita is for appearance, or for production of property, before a Court and it is proved to the satisfaction of that Court, or of any Court to which the case has subsequently been transferred, that the bond has been forfeited;

in respect of any other bond under this Sanhita, it is proved to the satisfaction of the Court by which the bond was taken, or of any Court to which the case has subsequently been transferred, or of the Court of any Magistrate of the first class, that the bond has been forfeited,

A condition in a bond for appearance, or for production of property, before a Court shall be construed as including a condition for appearance, or as the case may be, for production of property, before any Court to which the case may subsequently be transferred.

If sufficient cause is not shown and the penalty is not paid, the Court may proceed to recover the same as if such penalty were a fine imposed by it under this Sanhita: Provided that where such penalty is not paid and cannot be recovered in the manner aforesaid, the person so bound as surety shall be liable, by order of the Court ordering the recovery of the penalty, to imprisonment in civil jail for a term which may extend to six months.
The Court may, after recording its reasons for doing so, remit any portion of the penalty mentioned and enforce payment in part only.
Where a surety to a bond dies before the bond is forfeited, his estate shall be discharged from all liability in respect of the bond.

Where any person who has furnished security under section 125 or section 136 or section 401 is convicted of an offence the commission of which constitutes a breach of the conditions of his bond, or of a bond executed in lieu of his bond under section 494, a certified copy of the judgment of the Court by which he was convicted of such offence may be used as evidence in proceedings under this section against his surety or sureties, and, if such certified copy is so used, the Court shall presume that such offence was committed by him unless the contrary is proved.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Cancellation of bond and bail bond
content: .Without prejudice to the provisions of section 491, where a bond or bail bond under this Sanhita is for appearance of a person in a case and it is forfeited for breach of a condition, (a) the bond executed by such person as well as the bond, if any, executed by one or more of his sureties in that case shall stand cancelled; and (b) thereafter no such person shall be released only on his own bond in that case, if the police officer or the Court, as the case may be, for appearance before whom the bond was executed, is satisfied that there was no sufficient cause for the failure of the person bound by the bond to comply with its condition: Provided that subject to any other provisions of this Sanhita he may be released in that case upon the execution of a fresh personal bond for such sum of money and bond by one or more of such sureties as the police officer or the Court, as the case may be, thinks sufficient.
summary: If someone fails to meet the conditions of their bail bond, the bond and any surety bonds will be cancelled. This means the person will not be released on their own bond again for the same case. However, they can be released if they provide a new personal bond, either by themselves or one of their sureties, for a sufficient amount of money as decided by the police or court.

subsections.text: Without prejudice to the provisions of section 491, where a bond or bail bond under this Sanhita is for appearance of a person in a case and it is forfeited for breach of a condition,
the bond executed by such person as well as the bond, if any, executed by one or more of his sureties in that case shall stand cancelled;
and thereafter no such person shall be released only on his own bond in that case,

if the police officer or the Court, as the case may be, for appearance before whom the bond was executed, is satisfied that there was no sufficient cause for the failure of the person bound by the bond to comply with its condition:

Provided that subject to any other provisions of this Sanhita he may be released in that case upon the execution of a fresh personal bond for such sum of money and bond by one or more of such sureties as the police officer or the Court, as the case may be, thinks sufficient.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Procedure in case of insolvency or death of surety or when a bond is forfeited.
content: When any surety to a bail bond under this Sanhita becomes insolvent or dies, or when any bond is forfeited under the provisions of section 491, the Court by whose order such bond was taken, or a Magistrate of the first class may order the person from whom such security was demanded to furnish fresh security in accordance with the directions of the original order, and if such security is not furnished, such Court or Magistrate may proceed as if there had been a default in complying with such original order.
summary: If the person who guaranteed the bail (the surety) becomes bankrupt, dies, or if the bail bond is cancelled, the court that originally ordered the bail can ask the person who was required to provide the security to give new security. If this new security is not provided, the court can treat it as if the original order was not followed, and take further action accordingly.

subsections.text: When any surety to a bail bond under this Sanhita becomes insolvent or dies, or when any bond is forfeited under the provisions of section 491, the Court by whose order such bond was taken, or a Magistrate of the first class may order the person from whom such security was demanded to furnish fresh security in accordance with the directions of the original order, and if such security is not furnished, such Court or Magistrate may proceed as if there had been a default in complying with such original order.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Bond required from child.
content: When the person required by any Court, or officer to execute a bond is a child, such Court or officer may accept, in lieu thereof, a bond executed by a surety or sureties only.
summary: When a court or government officer asks a child to sign a bond, they can accept a bond signed by a responsible adult (or adults) instead, who promises to take care of the child's responsibilities.

subsections.text: When the person required by any Court, or officer to execute a bond is a child, such Court or officer may accept, in lieu thereof, a bond executed by a surety or sureties only.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Appeal from orders under section 491.
content: All orders passed under section 491 shall be appealable, (i) in the case of an order made by a Magistrate, to the Sessions Judge; (ii) in the case of an order made by a Court of Session, to the Court to which an appeal lies from an order made by such Court.
summary:
If a court makes a decision under Section 491 of the BNSS, you can appeal against that decision. 

- If the decision was made by a Magistrate (a lower-level court official), you can appeal to the Sessions Judge (a higher-level court official).
- If the decision was made by a Court of Session (a higher-level court), you can appeal to the next higher court where appeals from that court are usually heard.

subsections.text: All orders passed under section 491 shall be appealable,
in the case of an order made by a Magistrate, to the Sessions Judge;
in the case of an order made by a Court of Session, to the Court to which an appeal lies from an order made by such Court.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXV: PROVISIONS AS TO BAIL AND BONDS
title: Power to direct levy of amount due on certain recognizances.
content: The High Court or Court of Session may direct any Magistrate to levy the amount due on a bond for appearance or attendance at such High Court or Court of Session. CHAPTER XXXVI DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY
summary: If someone has given a bond to appear in court, but they fail to show up, the High Court or a Court of Session can order a local Magistrate to collect the amount that was promised in the bond. This is to ensure that the person who failed to appear in court is held accountable for their promise.

subsections.text: The High Court or Court of Session may direct any Magistrate to levy the amount due on a bond for appearance or attendance at such High Court or Court of Session.
DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVI: DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY
title: Order for custody and disposal of property pending trial in certain cases.
content: (1) When any property is produced before any Criminal Court or the Magistrate empowered to take cognizance or commit the case for trial during any investigation, inquiry or trial, the Court or the Magistrate may make such order as it thinks fit for the proper custody of such property pending the conclusion of the investigation, inquiry 165 or trial, and, if the property is subject to speedy and natural decay, or if it is otherwise expedient so to do, the Court or the Magistrate may, after recording such evidence as it thinks necessary, order it to be sold or otherwise disposed of. Explanation.For the purposes of this section, “property” includes (a) property of any kind or document which is produced before the Court or which is in its custody; (b) any property regarding which an offence appears to have been committed or which appears to have been used for the commission of any offence. (2) The Court or the Magistrate shall, within a period of fourteen days from the production of the property referred to in sub-section (1) before it, prepare a statement of such property containing its description in such form and manner as the State Government may, by rules, provide. (3) The Court or the Magistrate shall cause to be taken the photograph and if necessary, videograph on mobile phone or any electronic media, of the property referred to in sub-section (1). (4) The statement prepared under sub-section (2) and the photograph or the videography taken under sub-section (3) shall be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under the Sanhita. (5) The Court or the Magistrate shall, within a period of thirty days after the statement has been prepared under sub-section (2) and the photograph or the videography has been taken under sub-section (3), order the disposal, destruction, confiscation or delivery of the property in the manner specified hereinafter.
summary:
When a property is involved in a crime and is brought to court, the court can make decisions about who should take care of it until the case is solved. If the property is perishable or it's better to sell it, the court can order it to be sold or disposed of. 

The court must create a list of the property, including its description, within 14 days. They will also take photos and videos of the property. This information will be used as evidence in the case. 

After 30 days, the court will decide what to do with the property, such as giving it back to its owner, destroying it, or keeping it as evidence.

subsections.text: When any property is produced before any Criminal Court or the Magistrate empowered to take cognizance or commit the case for trial during any investigation, inquiry or trial, the Court or the Magistrate may make such order as it thinks fit for the proper custody of such property pending the conclusion of the investigation, inquiry 165 or trial, and, if the property is subject to speedy and natural decay, or if it is otherwise expedient so to do, the Court or the Magistrate may, after recording such evidence as it thinks necessary, order it to be sold or otherwise disposed of.

For the purposes of this section, “property” includes (a) property of any kind or document which is produced before the Court or which is in its custody; (b) any property regarding which an offence appears to have been committed or which appears to have been used for the commission of any offence.

The Court or the Magistrate shall, within a period of fourteen days from the production of the property referred to in sub-section (1) before it, prepare a statement of such property containing its description in such form and manner as the State Government may, by rules, provide.
The Court or the Magistrate shall cause to be taken the photograph and if necessary, videograph on mobile phone or any electronic media, of the property referred to in sub-section (1).
The statement prepared under sub-section (2) and the photograph or the videography taken under sub-section (3) shall be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under the Sanhita.

The Court or the Magistrate shall, within a period of thirty days after the statement has been prepared under sub-section (2) and the photograph or the videography has been taken under sub-section (3), order the disposal, destruction, confiscation or delivery of the property in the manner specified hereinafter.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVI: DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY
title: Order for disposal of property at conclusion of trial.
content: (1) When an investigation, inquiry or trial in any criminal case is concluded, the Court or the Magistrate may make such order as it thinks fit for the disposal, by destruction, confiscation or delivery to any person claiming to be entitled to possession thereof or otherwise, of any property or document produced before it or in its custody, or regarding which any offence appears to have been committed, or which has been used for the commission of any offence. (2) An order may be made under sub-section (1) for the delivery of any property to any person claiming to be entitled to the possession thereof, without any condition or on condition that he executes a bond, with or without securities, to the satisfaction of the Court or the Magistrate, engaging to restore such property to the Court if the order made under sub-section (1) is modified or set aside on appeal or revision. (3) A Court of Session may, instead of itself making an order under sub-section (1), direct the property to be delivered to the Chief Judicial Magistrate, who shall thereupon deal with it in the manner provided in sections 503, 504 and 505. (4) Except where the property is livestock or is subject to speedy and natural decay, or where a bond has been executed in pursuance of sub-section (2), an order made under sub-section (1) shall not be carried out for two months, or when an appeal is presented, until such appeal has been disposed of. (5) In this section, the term “property” includes, in the case of property regarding which an offence appears to have been committed, not only such property as has been originally in the possession or under the control of any party, but also any property into or for which the same may have been converted or exchanged, and anything acquired by such conversion or exchange, whether immediately or otherwise.
summary:
When a court case is over, the court can decide what to do with any property or documents that were involved in the case. They can choose to:

- Destroy the property
- Confiscate (take) the property
- Return the property to someone who claims to own it
- Make the person who gets the property promise to return it if the court's decision is changed on appeal

The court can also ask the Chief Judicial Magistrate to handle the property if it's too complicated. If the court decides to return the property, it usually can't be done right away. The court will wait at least two months or until any appeals are decided. This is to make sure the decision is final before anything is done with the property.

The term "property" in this section includes not just the original item, but also anything that was made from it or exchanged for it, even if it was done indirectly.

subsections.text: When an investigation, inquiry or trial in any criminal case is concluded, the Court or the Magistrate may make such order as it thinks fit for the disposal, by destruction, confiscation or delivery to any person claiming to be entitled to possession thereof or otherwise, of any property or document produced before it or in its custody, or regarding which any offence appears to have been committed, or which has been used for the commission of any offence.

An order may be made under sub-section (1) for the delivery of any property to any person claiming to be entitled to the possession thereof, without any condition or on condition that he executes a bond, with or without securities, to the satisfaction of the Court or the Magistrate, engaging to restore such property to the Court if the order made under sub-section (1) is modified or set aside on appeal or revision.

A Court of Session may, instead of itself making an order under sub-section (1), direct the property to be delivered to the Chief Judicial Magistrate, who shall thereupon deal with it in the manner provided in sections 503, 504 and 505.

Except where the property is livestock or is subject to speedy and natural decay, or where a bond has been executed in pursuance of sub-section (2), an order made under sub-section (1) shall not be carried out for two months, or when an appeal is presented, until such appeal has been disposed of.

In this section, the term “property” includes, in the case of property regarding which an offence appears to have been committed, not only such property as has been originally in the possession or under the control of any party, but also any property into or for which the same may have been converted or exchanged,
and anything acquired by such conversion or exchange, whether immediately or otherwise.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVI: DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY
title: Payment to innocent purchaser of money found on accused.
content: When any person is convicted of any offence which includes, or amounts to, theft or receiving stolen property, and it is proved that any other person bought the stolen property from him without knowing or having reason to believe that the same was stolen, and that any money has on his arrest been taken out of the possession of the convicted person, the Court may, on the application of such purchaser and on the restitution of the stolen property to the person entitled to the possession thereof, order that out of such money a sum not exceeding the price paid by such purchaser be delivered to him within six months from the date of such order. 166
summary:
If someone is found guilty of stealing or buying stolen goods, and another person bought the stolen item from them without knowing it was stolen, the court may help the second person get back some of their money. 

To do this, the second person must apply to the court and return the stolen item to its rightful owner. If the court agrees, they can order that some of the money found on the guilty person be given to the second person, up to the amount they originally paid for the item. This must be done within six months of the court's order.

subsections.text: When any person is convicted of any offence which includes, or amounts to, theft or receiving stolen property, and it is proved that any other person bought the stolen property from him without knowing or having reason to believe that the same was stolen, and that any money has on his arrest been taken out of the possession of the convicted person, the Court may, on the application of such purchaser and on the restitution of the stolen property to the person entitled to the possession thereof, order that out of such money a sum not exceeding the price paid by such purchaser be delivered to him within six months from the date of such order.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVI: DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY
title: Appeal against orders under section 498 or section 499.
content: (1) Any person aggrieved by an order made by a Court or Magistrate under section 498 or section 499, may appeal against it to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from convictions by the former Court. (2) On such appeal, the Appellate Court may direct the order to be stayed pending disposal of the appeal, or may modify, alter or annul the order and make any further orders that may be just. (3) The powers referred to in sub-section (2) may also be exercised by a Court of appeal, confirmation or revision while dealing with the case in which the order referred to in sub-section (1) was made.
summary:
If you're unhappy with a court or magistrate's decision under sections 498 or 499 of the BNSS, you can appeal to a higher court. This higher court can:

- Stop the original order from being enforced until your appeal is decided.
- Change, cancel, or modify the original order.
- Make new orders that are fair.

This power to change or cancel the original order can also be used by a higher court when it's reviewing a case where the original order was made.

subsections.text: Any person aggrieved by an order made by a Court or Magistrate under section 498 or section 499, may appeal against it to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from convictions by the former Court.
On such appeal, the Appellate Court may direct the order to be stayed pending disposal of the appeal, or may modify, alter or annul the order and make any further orders that may be just.
The powers referred to in sub-section (2) may also be exercised by a Court of appeal, confirmation or revision while dealing with the case in which the order referred to in sub-section (1) was made.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVI: DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY
title: Destruction of libellous and other matter.
content: (1) On a conviction under section 294, section 295, or sub-sections (3) and (4) of section 356 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, the Court may order the destruction of all the copies of the thing in respect of which the conviction was had, and which are in the custody of the Court or remain in the possession or power of the person convicted. (2) The Court may, in like manner, on a conviction under section 274, section 275, section 276 or section 277 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, order the food, drink, drug or medical preparation in respect of which the conviction was had, to be destroyed.
summary: If someone is found guilty of spreading false information that hurts someone's reputation (libel) or of making or selling something that is harmful to people's health, the court can order the destruction of all copies of the material or the product itself. This includes any copies in the court's possession or in the possession of the person who was found guilty.

subsections.text: On a conviction under section 294, section 295, or sub-sections (3) and (4) of section 356 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, the Court may order the destruction of all the copies of the thing in respect of which the conviction was had, and which are in the custody of the Court or remain in the possession or power of the person convicted.

The Court may, in like manner, on a conviction under section 274, section 275, section 276 or section 277 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, order the food, drink, drug or medical preparation in respect of which the conviction was had, to be destroyed.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVI: DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY
title: Power to restore possession of immovable property.
content: (1) When a person is convicted of an offence by use of criminal force or show of force or by criminal intimidation, and it appears to the Court that, by such use of force or show of force or intimidation, any person has been dispossessed of any immovable property, the Court may, if it thinks fit, order that possession of the same be restored to that person after evicting by force, if necessary, any other person who may be in possession of the property: Provided that no such order shall be made by the Court more than one month after the date of the conviction. (2) Where the Court trying the offence has not made an order under sub-section (1), the Court of appeal, confirmation or revision may, if it thinks fit, make such order while disposing of the appeal, reference or revision, as the case may be. (3) Where an order has been made under sub-section (1), the provisions of section 500 shall apply in relation thereto as they apply in relation to an order under section 499. (4) No order made under this section shall prejudice any right or interest to or in such immovable property which any person may be able to establish in a civil suit.
summary:
If someone uses force or intimidation to take away someone else's property, the court can order the property to be returned to its rightful owner. This can happen if the person who took the property is found guilty of a crime. The court can also force the person who is currently in possession of the property to leave if necessary.

This order can be made by the court that tried the case, or by a higher court if the case is appealed. However, the court must make this decision within one month of the person being found guilty.

Once the order is made, the court will follow certain procedures to ensure it is carried out. The order cannot affect any other rights or claims that people may have to the property, and can be challenged in a civil court if necessary.

subsections.text: When a person is convicted of an offence by use of criminal force or show of force or by criminal intimidation, and it appears to the Court that, by such use of force or show of force or intimidation, any person has been dispossessed of any immovable property, the Court may, if it thinks fit, order that possession of the same be restored to that person after evicting by force, if necessary, any other person who may be in possession of the property: Provided that no such order shall be made by the Court more than one month after the date of the conviction.

Where the Court trying the offence has not made an order under sub-section (1), the Court of appeal, confirmation or revision may, if it thinks fit, make such order while disposing of the appeal, reference or revision, as the case may be.
Where an order has been made under sub-section (1), the provisions of section 500 shall apply in relation thereto as they apply in relation to an order under section 499.
No order made under this section shall prejudice any right or interest to or in such immovable property which any person may be able to establish in a civil suit.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVI: DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY
title: Procedure by police upon seizure of property.
content: (1) Whenever the seizure of property by any police officer is reported to a Magistrate under the provisions of this Sanhita, and such property is not produced before a Criminal Court during an inquiry or trial, the Magistrate may make such order as he thinks fit respecting the disposal of such property or the delivery of such property to the person entitled to the possession thereof, or if such person cannot be ascertained, respecting the custody and production of such property. (2) If the person so entitled is known, the Magistrate may order the property to be delivered to him on such conditions (if any) as the Magistrate thinks fit and if such person is unknown, the Magistrate may detain it and shall, in such case, issue a proclamation specifying the articles of which such property consists, and requiring any person who may have a claim thereto, to appear before him and establish his claim within six months from the date of such proclamation.
summary:
When the police seize any property, they must inform a Magistrate about it. If the property is not used as evidence in court, the Magistrate decides what to do with it. 

If the owner of the property is known, the Magistrate can return it to them with some conditions if needed. 

If the owner is unknown, the Magistrate keeps the property and issues a public notice stating what the property is and asks anyone who claims it to appear in court within six months to prove their ownership.

subsections.text: Whenever the seizure of property by any police officer is reported to a Magistrate under the provisions of this Sanhita, and such property is not produced before a Criminal Court during an inquiry or trial, the Magistrate may make such order as he thinks fit respecting the disposal of such property or the delivery of such property to the person entitled to the possession thereof, or if such person cannot be ascertained, respecting the custody and production of such property.

If the person so entitled is known, the Magistrate may order the property to be delivered to him on such conditions (if any) as the Magistrate thinks fit and if such person is unknown, the Magistrate may detain it and shall, in such case, issue a proclamation specifying the articles of which such property consists, and requiring any person who may have a claim thereto, to appear before him and establish his claim within six months from the date of such proclamation.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVI: DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY
title: Procedure where no claimant appears within six months.
content: (1) If no person within such period establishes his claim to such property, and if the person in whose possession such property was found is unable to show that it was legally acquired by him, the Magistrate may by order direct that such property shall be at the disposal of the State Government and may be sold by that Government and the proceeds of such sale shall be dealt with in such manner as the State Government may, by rules, provide. 167 (2) An appeal shall lie against any such order to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from convictions by the Magistrate.
summary: If no one claims a lost or found property within six months, the Magistrate can decide what to do with it. If the person who found the property can't prove they got it legally, the Magistrate can take it away and sell it. The money from the sale will be handled according to the rules set by the State Government. If someone disagrees with the Magistrate's decision, they can appeal to a higher court.

subsections.text: If no person within such period establishes his claim to such property, and if the person in whose possession such property was found is unable to show that it was legally acquired by him, the Magistrate may by order direct that such property shall be at the disposal of the State Government and may be sold by that Government and the proceeds of such sale shall be dealt with in such manner as the State Government may, by rules, provide.
An appeal shall lie against any such order to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from convictions by the Magistrate.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVI: DISPOSAL OF PROPERTY
title: Power to sell perishable property.
content: If the person entitled to the possession of such property is unknown or absent and the property is subject to speedy and natural decay, or if the Magistrate to whom its seizure is reported is of opinion that its sale would be for the benefit of the owner, or that the value of such property is less than ten thousand rupees, the Magistrate may at any time direct it to be sold; and the provisions of sections 503 and 504 shall, as nearly as may be practicable, apply to the net proceeds of such sale. CHAPTER XXXVII IRREGULAR PROCEEDINGS
summary: If the rightful owner of a perishable item is unknown or not available, the court can sell it to prevent it from spoiling or losing value. This can happen if the court thinks selling the item will benefit the owner, or if the item's value is less than 10,000 rupees. The court can sell the item at any time and use the proceeds from the sale according to the rules mentioned in sections 503 and 504.

subsections.text: If the person entitled to the possession of such property is unknown or absent and the property is subject to speedy and natural decay, or if the Magistrate to whom its seizure is reported is of opinion that its sale would be for the benefit of the owner, or that the value of such property is less than ten thousand rupees,
the Magistrate may at any time direct it to be sold;
and the provisions of sections 503 and 504 shall, as nearly as may be practicable, apply to the net proceeds of such sale.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVII: IRREGULAR PROCEEDINGS
title: Irregularities which do not vitiate proceedings.
content: If any Magistrate not empowered by law to do any of the following things, namely: (a) to issue a search-warrant under section 97; (b) to order, under section 174, the police to investigate an offence; (c) to hold an inquest under section 196; (d) to issue process under section 207, for the apprehension of a person within his local jurisdiction who has committed an offence outside the limits of such jurisdiction; (e) to take cognizance of an offence under clause (a) or clause (b) of sub-section (1) of section 210; (f) to make over a case under sub-section (2) of section 212; (g) to tender a pardon under section 343; (h) to recall a case and try it himself under section 450; or (i) to sell property under section 504 or section 505, erroneously in good faith does that thing, his proceedings shall not be set aside merely on the ground of his not being so empowered.
summary: If a Magistrate makes a mistake while performing certain tasks, such as issuing a search warrant or ordering an investigation, but does so in good faith and without knowing they don't have the authority to do so, their actions will not be cancelled just because of this mistake.

subsections.text: If any Magistrate not empowered by law to do any of the following things, namely:
(a) to issue a search-warrant under section 97;
(b) to order, under section 174, the police to investigate an offence;
(c) to hold an inquest under section 196;
(d) to issue process under section 207, for the apprehension of a person within his local jurisdiction who has committed an offence outside the limits of such jurisdiction;
(e) to take cognizance of an offence under clause (a) or clause (b) of sub-section (1) of section 210;
(f) to make over a case under sub-section (2) of section 212;
(g) to tender a pardon under section 343;
(h) to recall a case and try it himself under section 450;
(i) to sell property under section 504 or section 505,
erroneously in good faith does that thing, his proceedings shall not be set aside merely on the ground of his not being so empowered.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVII: IRREGULAR PROCEEDINGS
title: Irregularities which vitiate proceedings.
content: If any Magistrate, not being empowered by law in this behalf, does any of the following things, namely: (a) attaches and sells property under section 85; (b) issues a search-warrant for a document, parcel or other things in the custody of a postal authority; (c) demands security to keep the peace; (d) demands security for good behaviour; (e) discharges a person lawfully bound to be of good behaviour; (f) cancels a bond to keep the peace; (g) makes an order for maintenance; (h) makes an order under section 152 as to a local nuisance; (i) prohibits, under section 162, the repetition or continuance of a public nuisance; (j) makes an order under Part C or Part D of Chapter XI; (k) takes cognizance of an offence under clause (c) of sub-section (1) of section 210; (l) tries an offender; 168 (m) tries an offender summarily; (n) passes a sentence, under section 364, on proceedings recorded by another Magistrate; (o) decides an appeal; (p) calls, under section 438, for proceedings; or (q) revises an order passed under section 491, his proceedings shall be void.
summary:
If a Magistrate does any of the following actions without the proper authority, the entire process will be considered invalid:

- Attaching and selling someone's property without the right to do so.
- Issuing a search warrant for a document or item in the custody of the postal authority.
- Demanding security from someone to keep the peace or for good behavior without the right to do so.
- Discharging someone who was lawfully required to behave well.
- Canceling a bond that was meant to keep the peace.
- Making an order for maintenance without the authority to do so.
- Making an order about a local nuisance or public disturbance without the authority.
- Prohibiting the repetition or continuance of a public nuisance without the authority.
- Trying a case or making a decision in certain situations without the authority.
- Trying a case without following the proper procedures.
- Passing a sentence based on someone else's recorded proceedings.
- Deciding an appeal without the authority to do so.
- Calling for proceedings or revising an order without the authority.

In all these cases, the entire process will be considered invalid and will have to be done again from the beginning.

subsections.text: If any Magistrate, not being empowered by law in this behalf, does any of the following things, namely:
attaches and sells property under section 85
issues a search-warrant for a document, parcel or other things in the custody of a postal authority
demands security to keep the peace
demands security for good behaviour
discharges a person lawfully bound to be of good behaviour
cancels a bond to keep the peace
makes an order for maintenance
makes an order under section 152 as to a local nuisance
prohibits, under section 162, the repetition or continuance of a public nuisance
makes an order under Part C or Part D of Chapter XI
takes cognizance of an offence under clause (c) of sub-section (1) of section 210
tries an offender
tries an offender summarily
passes a sentence, under section 364, on proceedings recorded by another Magistrate
decides an appeal
calls, under section 438, for proceedings
revises an order passed under section 491

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVII: IRREGULAR PROCEEDINGS
title: Proceedings in wrong place.
content: No finding, sentence or order of any Criminal Court shall be set aside merely on the ground that the inquiry, trial or other proceedings in the course of which it was arrived at or passed, took place in a wrong sessions division, district, sub-division or other local area, unless it appears that such error has in fact occasioned a failure of justice.
summary: If a court in India makes a decision or passes a sentence, it won't be cancelled just because the trial or hearing took place in the wrong area, such as a different district or sub-division. However, the decision can be overturned if it's proven that the mistake actually caused an unfair outcome in the case.

subsections.text: No finding, sentence or order of any Criminal Court shall be set aside merely on the ground that the inquiry, trial or other proceedings in the course of which it was arrived at or passed, took place in a wrong sessions division, district, sub-division or other local area, unless it appears that such error has in fact occasioned a failure of justice.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVII: IRREGULAR PROCEEDINGS
title: Non-compliance with provisions of section 183 or section 316.
content: (1) If any Court before which a confession or other statement of an accused person recorded, or purporting to be recorded under section 183 or section 316, is tendered, or has been received, in evidence finds that any of the provisions of either of such sections have not been complied with by the Magistrate recording the statement, it may, notwithstanding anything contained in section 94 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, take evidence in regard to such non-compliance, and may, if satisfied that such non-compliance has not injured the accused in his defence on the merits and that he duly made the statement recorded, admit such statement. (2) The provisions of this section apply to Courts of appeal, reference and revision.
summary:
If a court is presented with a confession or statement made by an accused person under sections 183 or 316 of the BNSS, and it finds out that the Magistrate who recorded the statement did not follow the required procedures, the court can still consider the statement as evidence. 

However, the court will only do this if it's convinced that not following the procedures did not harm the accused's defense and that the accused made the statement willingly. This rule applies to all courts, including those that hear appeals, references, or revisions.

subsections.text: If any Court before which a confession or other statement of an accused person recorded, or purporting to be recorded under section 183 or section 316, is tendered, or has been received, in evidence finds that any of the provisions of either of such sections have not been complied with by the Magistrate recording the statement, it may, notwithstanding anything contained in section 94 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, take evidence in regard to such non-compliance, and may, if satisfied that such non-compliance has not injured the accused in his defence on the merits and that he duly made the statement recorded, admit such statement.
The provisions of this section apply to Courts of appeal, reference and revision.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVII: IRREGULAR PROCEEDINGS
title: Effect of omission to frame, or absence of, or error in, charge.
content: (1) No finding, sentence or order by a Court of competent jurisdiction shall be deemed invalid merely on the ground that no charge was framed or on the ground of any error, omission or irregularity in the charge including any misjoinder of charges, unless, in the opinion of the Court of appeal, confirmation or revision, a failure of justice has in fact been occasioned thereby. (2) If the Court of appeal, confirmation or revision, is of opinion that a failure of justice has in fact been occasioned, it may, (a) in the case of an omission to frame a charge, order that a charge be framed, and that the trial be recommenced from the point immediately after the framing of the charge; (b) in the case of an error, omission or irregularity in the charge, direct a new trial to be had upon a charge framed in whatever manner it thinks fit: Provided that if the Court is of opinion that the facts of the case are such that no valid charge could be preferred against the accused in respect of the facts proved, it shall quash the conviction.
summary:
If a court finds that a charge was not framed correctly, or there was an error in the charge, it won't automatically cancel the court's decision. However, if the higher court thinks that this mistake has caused an injustice, it can take action.

If the higher court thinks an injustice has occurred because no charge was framed, it can order the court to frame a charge and then restart the trial from where the charge was framed.

If the higher court thinks an injustice has occurred because of an error in the charge, it can order a new trial with a new charge.

But if the higher court thinks that even with a new charge, the accused can't be convicted fairly, it can cancel the conviction.

subsections.text: No finding, sentence or order by a Court of competent jurisdiction shall be deemed invalid merely on the ground that no charge was framed or on the ground of any error, omission or irregularity in the charge including any misjoinder of charges, unless, in the opinion of the Court of appeal, confirmation or revision, a failure of justice has in fact been occasioned thereby.
If the Court of appeal, confirmation or revision, is of opinion that a failure of justice has in fact been occasioned, it may,
in the case of an omission to frame a charge, order that a charge be framed, and that the trial be recommenced from the point immediately after the framing of the charge;
in the case of an error, omission or irregularity in the charge, direct a new trial to be had upon a charge framed in whatever manner it thinks fit:
Provided that if the Court is of opinion that the facts of the case are such that no valid charge could be preferred against the accused in respect of the facts proved, it shall quash the conviction.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVII: IRREGULAR PROCEEDINGS
title: Finding or sentence when reversible by reason of error, omission or irregularity.
content: (1) Subject to the provisions hereinbefore contained, no finding, sentence or order passed by a Court of competent jurisdiction shall be reversed or altered by a Court of appeal, confirmation of revision on account of any error, omission or irregularity in the complaint, summons, warrant, proclamation, order, judgment or other proceedings before or during trial or in any inquiry or other proceedings under this Sanhita, or any error, or irregularity in any sanction for the prosecution, unless in the opinion of that Court, a failure of justice has in fact been occasioned thereby. (2) In determining whether any error, omission or irregularity in any proceeding under this Sanhita, or any error, or irregularity in any sanction for the prosecution has occasioned a failure of justice, the Court shall have regard to the fact whether the objection could and should have been raised at an earlier stage in the proceedings. 169
summary:
If a court makes a mistake or there's an error in the process of a trial or investigation, the decision can be changed or reversed. However, this can only happen if the mistake actually caused an unfair outcome. 

To decide if the mistake caused an unfair outcome, the court will consider whether the mistake could have been pointed out earlier in the process. If it could have been pointed out earlier, the court might not change the decision.

subsections.text: Subject to the provisions hereinbefore contained, no finding, sentence or order passed by a Court of competent jurisdiction shall be reversed or altered by a Court of appeal, confirmation of revision on account of any error, omission or irregularity in the complaint, summons, warrant, proclamation, order, judgment or other proceedings before or during trial or in any inquiry or other proceedings under this Sanhita, or any error, or irregularity in any sanction for the prosecution, unless in the opinion of that Court, a failure of justice has in fact been occasioned thereby.

In determining whether any error, omission or irregularity in any proceeding under this Sanhita, or any error, or irregularity in any sanction for the prosecution has occasioned a failure of justice, the Court shall have regard to the fact whether the objection could and should have been raised at an earlier stage in the proceedings.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVII: IRREGULAR PROCEEDINGS
title: Defect or error not to make attachment unlawful.
content: No attachment made under this Sanhita shall be deemed unlawful, nor shall any person making the same be deemed a trespasser, on account of any defect or want of form in the summons, conviction, writ of attachment or other proceedings relating thereto. CHAPTER XXXVIII LIMITATION FOR TAKING COGNIZANCE OF CERTAIN OFFENCES
summary: If a court orders the attachment of someone's property due to a debt or a crime, it won't be considered wrong or illegal even if there's a small mistake in the process, such as a wrong summons or incomplete paperwork. This means that the person who made the attachment won't be considered a trespasser, and the attachment itself will still be valid.

subsections.text: No attachment made under this Sanhita shall be deemed unlawful, nor shall any person making the same be deemed a trespasser, on account of any defect or want of form in the summons, conviction, writ of attachment or other proceedings relating thereto.
LIMITATION FOR TAKING COGNIZANCE OF CERTAIN OFFENCES

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVIII: LIMITATION FOR TAKING COGNIZANCE OF CERTAIN OFFENCES
title: Definitions.
content: For the purposes of this Chapter, unless the context otherwise requires, “period of limitation” means the period specified in section 514 for taking cognizance of an offence.
summary: In simple terms, the "period of limitation" refers to the time limit within which the police or authorities can take action against someone who has committed a crime, as specified in section 514 of the law.

subsections.text: For the purposes of this Chapter, unless the context otherwise requires, “period of limitation” means the period specified in section 514 for taking cognizance of an offence.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVIII: LIMITATION FOR TAKING COGNIZANCE OF CERTAIN OFFENCES
title: Bar to taking cognizance after lapse of period of limitation.
content: (1) Except as otherwise provided in this Sanhita, no Court shall take cognizance of an offence of the category specified in sub-section (2), after the expiry of the period of limitation. (2) The period of limitation shall be (a) six months, if the offence is punishable with fine only; (b) one year, if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for a term not exceeding one year; (c) three years, if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for a term exceeding one year but not exceeding three years. (3) For the purposes of this section, the period of limitation, in relation to offences which may be tried together, shall be determined with reference to the offence which is punishable with the more severe punishment or, as the case may be, the most severe punishment. Explanation.For the purpose of computing the period of limitation, the relevant date shall be the date of filing complaint under section 223 or the date of recording of information under section 173.
summary:
In simple words, this section of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS) says that a court cannot take action on a crime after a certain period of time has passed. The time limit depends on the type of crime and its punishment. 

- If a crime is punishable with a fine only, the court can take action within 6 months.
- If a crime is punishable with up to 1 year of imprisonment, the court can take action within 1 year.
- If a crime is punishable with more than 1 year but up to 3 years of imprisonment, the court can take action within 3 years.

The time starts counting from the date when a complaint is filed or when information about the crime is recorded. If multiple crimes can be tried together, the court will look at the most severe punishment to determine the time limit.

subsections.text: Except as otherwise provided in this Sanhita, no Court shall take cognizance of an offence of the category specified in sub-section (2), after the expiry of the period of limitation.

The period of limitation shall be (a) six months, if the offence is punishable with fine only; (b) one year, if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for a term not exceeding one year; (c) three years, if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for a term exceeding one year but not exceeding three years.

For the purposes of this section, the period of limitation, in relation to offences which may be tried together, shall be determined with reference to the offence which is punishable with the more severe punishment or, as the case may be, the most severe punishment.
For the purpose of computing the period of limitation, the relevant date shall be the date of filing complaint under section 223 or the date of recording of information under section 173.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVIII: LIMITATION FOR TAKING COGNIZANCE OF CERTAIN OFFENCES
title: Commencement of period of limitation.
content: (1) The period of limitation, in relation to an offender, shall commence, (a) on the date of the offence; or (b) where the commission of the offence was not known to the person aggrieved by the offence or to any police officer, the first day on which such offence comes to the knowledge of such person or to any police officer, whichever is earlier; or (c) where it is not known by whom the offence was committed, the first day on which the identity of the offender is known to the person aggrieved by the offence or to the police officer making investigation into the offence, whichever is earlier. (2) In computing the said period, the day from which such period is to be computed shall be excluded.
summary:
The period of time you have to take legal action against someone who has committed an offense starts from the date of the offense. However, if you didn't know about the offense, the time period starts from the day you found out about it. If you don't know who committed the offense, the time period starts from the day you find out who the offender is. 

When counting the time period, the day it starts from is not included.

subsections.text: The period of limitation, in relation to an offender, shall commence,
on the date of the offence;

where the commission of the offence was not known to the person aggrieved by the offence or to any police officer, the first day on which such offence comes to the knowledge of such person or to any police officer, whichever is earlier;

where it is not known by whom the offence was committed, the first day on which the identity of the offender is known to the person aggrieved by the offence or to the police officer making investigation into the offence, whichever is earlier.
In computing the said period, the day from which such period is to be computed shall be excluded.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVIII: LIMITATION FOR TAKING COGNIZANCE OF CERTAIN OFFENCES
title: Exclusion of time in certain cases.
content: (1) In computing the period of limitation, the time during which any person has been prosecuting with due diligence another prosecution, whether in a Court of first instance or in a Court of appeal or revision, against the offender, shall be excluded: Provided that no such exclusion shall be made unless the prosecution relates to the same facts and is prosecuted in good faith in a Court which from defect of jurisdiction or other cause of a like nature, is unable to entertain it. (2) Where the institution of the prosecution in respect of an offence has been stayed by an injunction or order, then, in computing the period of limitation, the period of the continuance of the injunction or order, the day on which it was issued or made, and the day on which it was withdrawn, shall be excluded. (3) Where notice of prosecution for an offence has been given, or where, under any law for the time being in force, the previous consent or sanction of the Government or any other authority is required for the institution of any prosecution for an offence, then, in computing the period of limitation, the period of 170 such notice or, as the case may be, the time required for obtaining such consent or sanction shall be excluded. Explanation.In computing the time required for obtaining the consent or sanction of the Government or any other authority, the date on which the application was made for obtaining the consent or sanction and the date of receipt of the order of the Government or other authority shall both be excluded. (4) In computing the period of limitation, the time during which the offender (a) has been absent from India or from any territory outside India which is under the administration of the Central Government; or (b) has avoided arrest by absconding or concealing himself, shall be excluded.
summary:
When calculating the time limit for a case, certain periods are excluded. These exclusions include:

- Time spent prosecuting another case against the same person in good faith, if the original court couldn't handle the case due to lack of jurisdiction or other similar reasons.

- Time during which a case was stayed due to an injunction or court order.

- Time spent waiting for a prosecution notice to be given or for government consent to prosecute.

- Time spent waiting for government consent or sanction to prosecute, which includes the date the application was made and the date the order was received.

- Time spent while the offender is absent from India or avoiding arrest by hiding.

subsections.text: In computing the period of limitation, the time during which any person has been prosecuting with due diligence another prosecution, whether in a Court of first instance or in a Court of appeal or revision, against the offender, shall be excluded: Provided that no such exclusion shall be made unless the prosecution relates to the same facts and is prosecuted in good faith in a Court which from defect of jurisdiction or other cause of a like nature, is unable to entertain it.

Where the institution of the prosecution in respect of an offence has been stayed by an injunction or order, then, in computing the period of limitation, the period of the continuance of the injunction or order, the day on which it was issued or made, and the day on which it was withdrawn, shall be excluded.

Where notice of prosecution for an offence has been given, or where, under any law for the time being in force, the previous consent or sanction of the Government or any other authority is required for the institution of any prosecution for an offence, then, in computing the period of limitation, the period of 170 such notice or, as the case may be, the time required for obtaining such consent or sanction shall be excluded.

In computing the time required for obtaining the consent or sanction of the Government or any other authority, the date on which the application was made for obtaining the consent or sanction and the date of receipt of the order of the Government or other authority shall both be excluded.

In computing the period of limitation, the time during which the offender (a) has been absent from India or from any territory outside India which is under the administration of the Central Government; or (b) has avoided arrest by absconding or concealing himself, shall be excluded.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVIII: LIMITATION FOR TAKING COGNIZANCE OF CERTAIN OFFENCES
title: Exclusion of date on which Court is closed.
content: Where the period of limitation expires on a day when the Court is closed, the Court may take cognizance on the day on which the Court reopens. Explanation.A Court shall be deemed to be closed on any day within the meaning of this section, if, during its normal working hours, it remains closed on that day.
summary: If the time limit for a case expires on a day when the court is closed, the court can start processing the case on the next day it reopens. This means if the court is not working on a particular day, the time limit is extended to the next working day.

subsections.text: Where the period of limitation expires on a day when the Court is closed, the Court may take cognizance on the day on which the Court reopens.
A Court shall be deemed to be closed on any day within the meaning of this section, if, during its normal working hours, it remains closed on that day.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVIII: LIMITATION FOR TAKING COGNIZANCE OF CERTAIN OFFENCES
title: Continuing offence.
content: In the case of a continuing offence, a fresh period of limitation shall begin to run at every moment of the time during which the offence continues.
summary: If a crime is happening continuously, such as someone stealing from a shop every day, the clock for filing a complaint or lawsuit starts over every day the crime continues. This means that even if the crime started a long time ago, the person who was wronged can still file a complaint or lawsuit as long as the crime is still happening.

subsections.text: In the case of a continuing offence, a fresh period of limitation shall begin to run at every moment of the time during which the offence continues.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXVIII: LIMITATION FOR TAKING COGNIZANCE OF CERTAIN OFFENCES
title: Extension of period of limitation in certain cases.
content: Notwithstanding anything contained in the foregoing provisions of this Chapter, any Court may take cognizance of an offence after the expiry of the period of limitation, if it is satisfied on the facts and in the circumstances of the case that the delay has been properly explained or that it is necessary so to do in the interests of justice. CHAPTER XXXIX MISCELLANEOUS
summary: If someone files a complaint or case after the time limit mentioned in the law has passed, the court can still consider it. This can happen if the person explains why they were late in filing the case and the court believes them, or if the court thinks it's fair to consider the case despite the delay.

subsections.text: Notwithstanding anything contained in the foregoing provisions of this Chapter, any Court may take cognizance of an offence after the expiry of the period of limitation, if it is satisfied on the facts and in the circumstances of the case that the delay has been properly explained or that it is necessary so to do in the interests of justice.
CHAPTER XXXIX MISCELLANEOUS

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIX: MISCELLANEOUS
title: Trials before High Courts.
content: When an offence is tried by the High Court otherwise than under section 447, it shall, in the trial of the offence, observe the same procedure as a Court of Sessions would observe if it were trying the case.
summary: When a case is tried in a High Court, it will follow the same procedure as a Court of Sessions would, if it were trying the case. This means the High Court will use the same rules and steps as a Court of Sessions, ensuring consistency in the trial process.

subsections.text: When an offence is tried by the High Court otherwise than under section 447, it shall, in the trial of the offence, observe the same procedure as a Court of Sessions would observe if it were trying the case.

chapter: CHAPTER XXXIX: MISCELLANEOUS
title: Delivery to commanding officers of persons liable to be tried by Court-martial.
content: (1) The Central Government may make rules consistent with this Sanhita and the Air Force Act, 1950 (45 of 1950), the Army Act, 1950 (46 of 1950), the Navy Act, 1957 (62 of 1957), and any other law, relating to the Armed Forces of the Union, for the time being in force, as to cases in which persons subject to army, naval or airforce law, or such other law, shall be tried by a Court to which this Sanhita applies, or by a Court-martial; and when any person is brought before a Magistrate and charged with an offence for which he is liable to be tried either by a Court to which this Sanhita applies or by a Court-martial, such Magistrate shall have regard to such rules, and shall in proper cases deliver him, together with a statement of the offence of which he is accused, to the commanding officer of the unit to which he belongs, or to the commanding officer of the nearest army, naval or air-force station, as the case may be, for the purpose of being tried by a Courtmartial. Explanation.In this section (a) “unit” includes a regiment, corps, ship, detachment, group, battalion or company; (b) “Court-martial” includes any Tribunal with the powers similar to those of a Court-martial constituted under the relevant law applicable to the Armed Forces of the Union. 171 (2) Every Magistrate shall, on receiving a written application for that purpose by the commanding officer of any unit or body of soldiers, sailors or airmen stationed or employed at any such place, use his utmost endeavours to apprehend and secure any person accused of such offence. (3) A High Court may, if it thinks fit, direct that a prisoner detained in any jail situate within the State be brought before a Court-martial for trial or to be examined touching any matter pending before the Courtmartial.
summary:
When a person in the military is accused of a crime, a Magistrate will decide if they should be tried by a regular court or a Court-martial. If the Magistrate decides a Court-martial is the right place, they will send the accused person to their commanding officer along with details of the crime they are accused of. 

The commanding officer is in charge of the military unit the accused person belongs to. The Magistrate must follow rules made by the Central Government for this process. 

If the commanding officer wants to bring someone to a Court-martial, they can ask the Magistrate to help. The Magistrate will try their best to find and arrest the accused person.
